title: Replace SUPABASE_USER_ID env var with JWT extraction in all 20+ Netlify functions
description:
All Netlify functions read process.env.SUPABASE_USER_ID as the user identity. This must be replaced with extractUserId(req) from netlify/lib/auth.js so each request is authenticated individually.

Functions requiring userId for DB ops: tasks.js, recommend.js, recommendations.js, evaluate-rules.js, collect-world-state.js, calendar-sync.js, ical-sync.js, ical-feeds.js, ical-save-url.js, schedule-task.js, notifications.js, auth-google-refresh.js, auth-google-status.js.

Auth gate only (no userId needed): intake.js, beads-show.js, beads-create.js, auth-google-start.js.

Exceptions: auth-google-callback.js (browser redirect, no Bearer token) and calendar-sync-scheduled.js (Netlify cron) are NOT updated here.

Also: recommend.js cache is already per-user via explicit .eq('user_id', userId) queries — no module-level cache to fix.

## Scenarios

- Given POST to /.netlify/functions/tasks with no Authorization header
  When the handler runs
  Then returns 401 immediately, no Supabase DB query is made

- Given POST with valid JWT belonging to user A
  When tasks are fetched from open_tasks
  Then only user A's tasks are returned (RLS via auth.uid())

- Given POST to /.netlify/functions/intake with no Authorization header
  When the auth gate runs
  Then returns 401, no Claude API call made

- Given recommend.js has cached data for user A and user B makes a request
  When the cache check runs with user B's userId
  Then cache miss (different user_id filter on recommendation_history), new Claude call

- Given an attacker sends a valid JWT from user A to a function that previously used SUPABASE_USER_ID
  When extractUserId returns user A's id and the query runs
  Then RLS filters to user A's data only — user B's data is not accessible

- Given recommend.js has a warm cache entry for user A, then user B makes a request
  When the cache lookup runs with userId as key
  Then cache miss for user B, new Claude call made — user A's recommendations not returned
close_reason: All 17 on-demand Netlify functions now use extractUserId(req) for auth. Three explicit exceptions kept: calendar-sync-scheduled (cron), auth-google-callback (browser redirect, life-3po), auth-google-refresh (Railway server-to-server). Committed in 6375198.

title: Implement netlify/lib/auth.js JWT extraction utility and apiFetch wrapper
description:
All Netlify functions read process.env.SUPABASE_USER_ID as the user identity. To support JWT-based auth, a shared utility is needed that extracts and verifies the user_id from the Authorization: Bearer <jwt> header, and a client-side apiFetch wrapper that injects the session token on every function call.

Create netlify/lib/auth.js exporting extractUserId(req): reads Authorization header, calls supabase.auth.getUser(token) with service role key, returns user.id or throws { status: 401 }.

Add apiFetch helper in the PWA: calls supabase.auth.getSession() on each request to get the current access token (handles auto-refresh) and injects Authorization: Bearer <token> header on all /.netlify/functions/* calls.

## Scenarios

- Given a Netlify function receives a request with valid Bearer JWT in Authorization header
  When extractUserId(req) is called
  Then returns the correct user_id UUID string

- Given a request with no Authorization header
  When extractUserId(req) is called
  Then throws an error with status 401

- Given a request with expired or malformed JWT
  When extractUserId(req) is called
  Then supabase.auth.getUser returns error, extractUserId throws 401

- Given the PWA makes a fetch to /.netlify/functions/tasks after AuthGuard provides a session
  When apiFetch wraps the call
  Then Authorization: Bearer <current access_token> header is present on the request

title: Implement AuthGuard PWA login screen (magic link sign-in)
description:
The app currently renders directly with no authentication check. Anyone with the Netlify URL has full access to all data and functions.

Add src/AuthGuard.jsx that on mount calls supabase.auth.getSession(). If no session, renders an email sign-in form that calls supabase.auth.signInWithOtp({ email }) and shows a Check your email confirmation state. Subscribes to supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange() for reactive session updates. When a session is present, renders {children}.

Update src/main.jsx to wrap LifeOrganizer with AuthGuard.

NOTE: Deploy ONLY after supabase db push (life-gmq migration) is verified.

## Scenarios

- Given a user visits the app without a Supabase session (incognito browser)
  When AuthGuard checks getSession()
  Then the sign-in form renders and the app content (tasks, calendar, beads) is NOT visible

- Given the user enters g.mofro@gmail.com and submits the sign-in form
  When supabase.auth.signInWithOtp fires
  Then UI shows Check your email confirmation state

- Given the user clicks the magic link in their email
  When Supabase OTP is verified and onAuthStateChange fires
  Then session is established and the app renders with Mo's tasks and data visible

- Given an authenticated user's session token expires
  When the app mounts or onAuthStateChange fires with SIGNED_OUT
  Then sign-in form re-renders, app content hidden

title: Supabase Auth migration: pre-insert Mo's auth user with existing data UUID
description:
All existing data rows carry user_id = 37b3da7e-de14-45e3-9969-e1350b8a6a30. When Supabase Auth is enabled, auth.uid() must equal this UUID for Mo's session or every existing row becomes inaccessible under RLS. This migration pre-inserts the auth user BEFORE AuthGuard is enabled.

DEPLOYMENT ORDER (CRITICAL):
1. supabase db push (applies this migration)
2. Verify: SELECT id FROM auth.users WHERE id = '37b3da7e-de14-45e3-9969-e1350b8a6a30'
3. netlify deploy --prod (enables AuthGuard in the PWA)
DO NOT deploy the PWA before this migration is verified.

## Scenarios

  When SELECT id, email FROM auth.users WHERE id = '37b3da7e-de14-45e3-9969-e1350b8a6a30'
  Then returns exactly one row with email g.mofro@gmail.com

  When db push runs again with the same file
  Then completes without error (ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING), existing row unchanged

- Given the migration succeeds and Mo signs in via magic link to g.mofro@gmail.com
  When auth.uid() is checked in a Supabase query
  Then auth.uid() = '37b3da7e-de14-45e3-9969-e1350b8a6a30' and all existing rows are visible

title: Fix Railway RCE: replace shell-exec in bd() helper with spawnSync + allowlist
description:
Railway server/index.js passes route params directly into execSync('/bin/bash -c ...'), making all five beads API routes (/api/beads/list, /api/beads/show/:id, /api/beads/claim/:id, /api/beads/close/:id, /api/beads/stats) vulnerable to shell injection RCE.

Fix: Replace execSync with spawnSync using a string array (no shell). Validate each param with a strict allowlist before passing: IDs must match /^[a-z][a-z0-9]*-[a-z0-9]+$/, status/priority must be from a fixed enum. Return 400 for invalid input before any process spawn. This change is in the Railway repo (server/index.js), not this repo, and requires a Railway redeploy.

## Scenarios

- Given an attacker sends GET /api/beads/show/'; echo INJECTED' to the Railway service
  When the handler validates the id param
  Then returns 400 (fails allowlist), no shell process spawned, no output leaked

- Given a valid beads ID 'life-abc' is sent to GET /api/beads/show/life-abc
  When the handler processes the request
  Then spawnSync('bd', ['show', 'life-abc']) executes correctly and returns the issue JSON

- Given POST /api/beads/list?status=open&priority=2
  When the handler validates status and priority params
  Then only enum-whitelisted values reach spawnSync; any other value returns 400

Requires Railway redeploy to take effect in production — will deploy when security-hardening branch merges.

title: Fix Railway BEADS_API_KEY — sync truncated key with Netlify
description:
The Railway CLI's variables display truncates long values. When we read back BEADS_API_KEY after setting it, we only saw 40 chars of the 64-char key. We then set Netlify to that truncated value — now they're out of sync and Railway returns 401 to the Context Collector.

Fix (once Railway outage at status.railway.com clears):

  railway variables set BEADS_API_KEY=dbd7d6233d3cc24f15b9437882a13a7fdf41914a4a5d3bd3a28ae6d2b6b88093

That key is already set on Netlify and documented in .env.local.

Verify:
  curl -s -H 'Authorization: Bearer dbd7d6233d3cc24f15b9437882a13a7fdf41914a4a5d3bd3a28ae6d2b6b88093' \
    https://life-organizer-production-4c1f.up.railway.app/api/beads/ready

Then confirm the Context Collector returns non-empty beadsReady:
  curl -s https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/collect-world-state | jq .beadsError

Should be null (not '401').

## Scenarios

- Given the Railway outage has cleared
  When railway variables set BEADS_API_KEY=dbd7d6...8093 is run
  Then Railway redeploys and GET /api/beads/ready with that key returns issue data (not 401)

- Given the key is set correctly on Railway
  When the Context Collector Netlify function runs
  Then beadsError is null and beadsReady contains the current ready issues from Beads
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix server/index.js for Linux/Railway compatibility
description:
Three changes required before server/index.js can run in a Linux container on Railway.

Why this exists:
server/index.js was written for macOS/zsh. Railway runs Linux containers where zsh is not present and PORT is injected by the platform. These must be fixed before the Beads Service can be deployed.

Changes required:
1. PORT: change hardcoded `const PORT = 3001` to `const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3001`
   (Railway injects PORT; hardcoded 3001 causes bind failure)

2. Shell: change all `shell: '/bin/zsh'` in execSync calls to `shell: '/bin/bash'`
   (zsh not present in node:20-slim Linux images; bash is always available)

3. Verify: BDG_DIR uses `resolve(homedir(), 'beads-global')` which correctly resolves
   to /root/beads-global in the container — no change needed here.

Acceptance criteria:
- `npm run server` still works locally on macOS after changes
- server/index.js passes startup validation in a Linux Docker container
- /api/health returns ok=true on Railway after deploy

Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

## Scenarios

- Given server/index.js is deployed to Railway
  When Railway starts the container and injects PORT=8080
  Then the server binds to port 8080 and /api/health returns ok=true

- Given server/index.js is running in a node:20-slim Linux container
  When the startup validation runs
  Then bd is found via @beads/bd (npm), no zsh-not-found error occurs

- Given the changes are applied
  When `npm run server` is run locally on macOS
  Then the server starts on port 3001 (PORT env var not set locally) with no errors

- Given a request hits /api/beads/ready on the deployed Railway service
  When bd executes in /bin/bash
  Then issues are returned correctly (same output as local macOS)
close_reason: PORT now reads process.env.PORT || 3001; all shell: '/bin/zsh' changed to '/bin/bash'; error messages updated. Verified: server starts locally on macOS, /api/health returns ok=true.

title: Context Collector v1: Beads + Tasks adapters writing to World State
description:
First version of the Context Collector: reads from the deployed Beads Service and Supabase tasks table, normalises, and writes a unified world state snapshot. This is the first data actually flowing through the new architecture.

What it does:
On trigger (app open or manual refresh — polling only in v1, Dolt hooks deferred to life-43k):
1. Call deployed Beads Service GET /api/beads/ready → upsert into Supabase beads_ready table
2. Read Supabase open_tasks WHERE status != 'completed' → compute derived fields
3. Derived fields: tasks_overdue, tasks_due_today, tasks_due_this_week
4. Expose everything via useWorldState React hook

Implementation:
- New Netlify function: netlify/functions/collect-world-state.js
  • Fetches from Railway (BEADS_API_KEY auth), upserts beads_ready rows in Supabase (SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY)
  • Returns full world state snapshot to caller
- New React hook: src/useWorldState.js
  • Calls the Netlify function on mount and on manual refresh
  • Returns { beadsReady, openTasks, derived, loading, error, refresh }
- Wire useWorldState into LifeOrganizer.jsx replacing useBeadsTasks

Note: Calendar data NOT in v1 (life-5eu). Dolt hook trigger NOT in v1 (life-43k).
Service role key stays server-side in Netlify function only — never in client code.

Depends on: life-xqz (deployed Beads Service ✓), life-57m (World State schema ✓)
Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

## Scenarios

- Given the Railway Beads Service is reachable and has ready issues
  When the app opens or the user triggers a manual refresh
  Then the beads_ready table in Supabase is upserted with current ready issues and useWorldState returns them to the UI

- Given open_tasks rows exist in Supabase with deadlines
  When the Context Collector runs
  Then tasks_overdue, tasks_due_today, and tasks_due_this_week are computed correctly and exposed via the useWorldState hook

- Given the Railway Beads Service is unreachable
  When the Context Collector attempts to fetch Beads data
  Then the last-known beads_ready rows are preserved in Supabase, an error is logged, and the UI renders stale data with a staleness indicator rather than crashing

- Given no open tasks exist in Supabase yet
  When the Context Collector runs for the first time
  Then all derived counts are 0 and the UI renders an empty state without errors
close_reason: Context Collector deployed and working in production. Fixed PostgREST in-filter syntax ('completed','cancelled' → unquoted) and applied beads_ready schema migration (status + issue_type columns). World State sync confirmed working at life-organizer-mo.netlify.app.

title: Design and provision Supabase World State schema
description:
Create the Supabase project and schema that serves as the shared World State store for all agents. This is the central dependency for the entire intelligence layer.

Why:
Without World State, the Context Collector has nowhere to write, the Rules Engine has nothing to evaluate, and no agent can share context. This is the single most blocking infrastructure dependency after the Beads Service.

Account setup:
Sign up at supabase.com via GitHub OAuth. Create project in nearest region (us-east-1). Free tier: 2 active projects, 500 MB DB, 5 GB egress — sufficient indefinitely.
WARNING: Auto-pause after 7 days inactivity on free tier. Resume is instant but scheduled crons will fail during pause.

Irrevocable schema decisions (cannot easily change after data exists):
- ALL timestamps: timestamptz — never plain timestamp
- ALL JSON: jsonb — never json (supports indexing)
- Primary keys: bigint generated by default as identity — not UUID
- EVERY table must have user_id uuid column (for RLS, even single-user now)
- EVERY table must have created_at + updated_at with moddatetime() trigger
- Enable RLS on ALL tables from day one

Auth model: Supabase Auth + JWT. Server-side (Netlify Functions, Railway) uses SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY. PWA client uses SUPABASE_ANON_KEY + user JWT. Service role key NEVER in client code.

Tables to create:
  calendar_snapshot (id, user_id, event_date, events jsonb, created_at, updated_at)
  open_tasks (id, user_id, title, status, priority, time_required_minutes, deadline, source, source_url, created_at, updated_at)
  beads_ready (id, user_id, issue_id, title, priority, blocked_by jsonb, synced_at)
  rules (id, user_id, name, condition_type, condition_config jsonb, priority_threshold, cooldown_minutes, total_fires, accepted_count, dismissed_count, deprecated_at, created_at, updated_at)
  notification_log (id, user_id, rule_id, payload jsonb, fired_at, user_action, action_at, action_latency_seconds)
  recommendation_history (id, user_id, content, world_state_snapshot jsonb, user_action, created_at)
  rule_suggestions (id, user_id, suggestion_type, target_rule_id, original_value jsonb, suggested_value jsonb, reasoning, confidence, approved, approved_at, applied_at, created_at)
  user_preferences (id, user_id, notifications_enabled, quiet_hours_start, quiet_hours_end, email, timezone, auto_apply_threshold, updated_at)

Implementation steps:
1. Create Supabase project.
2. Run schema SQL in Supabase SQL editor (add moddatetime extension, create tables, add indexes on user_id and timestamp columns, enable RLS, add RLS policies).
3. Seed rules table with 4 v1 seed rules from ARD v2.
4. Add to 1Password: SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY.
5. Add to local .env.local: VITE_SUPABASE_URL, VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY.

## Scenarios

- Given an empty Supabase project (just created)
  When the migration SQL runs in the Supabase SQL editor
  Then all 8 tables are created, RLS is enabled on each, moddatetime triggers are installed, and a test INSERT + SELECT through the anon key respects the user_id filter

- Given the World State schema is provisioned
  When a Netlify Function using the service role key inserts a row into open_tasks
  Then the row is visible server-side, but a client-side request using the anon key only sees rows where user_id matches auth.uid()

- Given the Supabase project on the free tier hasn't been accessed for 7 days
  When any app request hits Supabase
  Then the project resumes within ~2s and the request completes — scheduled cron failures during the pause window are expected and acceptable

- Given a row in any table is updated
  When the UPDATE completes
  Then the moddatetime() trigger fires and updated_at reflects the current timestamp
close_reason: Supabase project provisioned at vmjcqxuxltzskuudfsri.supabase.co. All 8 tables created with RLS, moddatetime triggers, and indexes. 4 seed rules inserted. Credentials in .env.local, Netlify, and Railway.

title: Deploy Beads Service as persistent cloud endpoint
description:
Deploy server/index.js as a persistent cloud service so cloud functions and the Context Collector can read Beads data without local machine access.

Why:
The current server/index.js runs on localhost:3001 during development. Cloud functions (Netlify, scheduled jobs) have no way to reach it. Deploying it as a persistent service eliminates the local/cloud divide for Beads data.

Host: Railway (confirmed). Always-on Node.js, ~$5/mo, simplest deploy story.

Prerequisites (must be complete first):
- life-6zf: Dolt remote configured (DONE — mofro/beads-global on DoltHub)
- life-men: server/index.js Linux compatibility fixes (PORT, shell)

Implementation:
1. Write Dockerfile in repo root:
   - Base image: node:20-slim
   - Install dolt binary (curl from github.com/dolthub/dolt/releases)
   - Run: npm install -g @beads/bd  (installs Linux bd binary — official npm package)
   - Copy server/index.js and package.json
   - Write startup script: bd init (if needed), bd dolt remote add, bd dolt pull, node server/index.js

2. Connect Railway to GitHub repo (life-organizer), set root directory, set start command.

3. Set Railway environment variables:
   - DOLT_REMOTE_USER=mofro
   - DOLT_REMOTE_PASSWORD=<DoltHub token from 1Password>
   - BEADS_API_KEY=<generate — store in 1Password>
   - SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY (once Supabase is set up)

4. Add BEADS_SERVICE_URL + BEADS_API_KEY to Netlify env vars and local .env.local.

5. Verify: GET /api/health returns ok=true from Railway public URL.

Note: write operations (claim, close) flow through this service → Dolt remote → local bd dolt pull keeps local in sync.

Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

## Scenarios

- Given the Dockerfile and railway.toml are committed to the repo and Railway is connected to the GitHub repo
  When Railway triggers a deploy from main
  Then the container builds successfully, the startup script runs bd init → dolt pull → node server/index.js, and GET /api/health returns {ok: true}

- Given the Beads Service container is running on Railway
  When a local `bd dolt push` syncs new issue data to DoltHub
  Then a subsequent GET /api/beads/ready on the Railway URL returns the updated issues (sync runs on that endpoint)

- Given the container starts for the first time with no existing beads data in the image
  When the startup script runs
  Then bd init creates ~/beads-global, dolt pull populates it from DoltHub, and the server starts cleanly with valid data

- Given DoltHub is unreachable when the container starts
  When the dolt pull in the startup script fails
  Then the server starts anyway with empty or stale local data and logs a warning — it does not crash
close_reason: Deployed to Railway at https://life-organizer-production-4c1f.up.railway.app — /api/health returns ok=true, bd 1.0.4, BEADS_DIR=/app confirmed. Auth middleware live.

title: EPIC: Configure Dolt remote for beads-global
description:
Set up a Dolt remote so beads-global state can be accessed from the cloud. This unblocks deploying the Beads Service.

Why this first:
The deployed Beads Service (server/index.js) needs to sync from a remote Dolt repo on startup. Without a configured remote, it can only serve whatever state was baked into the container at build time — useless for live data.

Implementation:
1. Evaluate DoltHub (managed, free tier, simple setup) vs. self-hosted Dolt server.
2. Create remote: bd dolt remote add origin <url>
3. Initial push: bd dolt push origin main
4. Verify: clone the remote on a separate machine, confirm issues are visible.
5. Add bd dolt push to the standard session-close workflow (already in CLAUDE.md).
6. Document remote URL in .notes and README.

Recommendation: DoltHub unless there are privacy concerns about issue content being in a managed service.

Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)
notes: RESOLVED: DoltHub public repo configured. Remote: https://doltremoteapi.dolthub.com/mofro/beads-global. POC uses DoltHub public (free). Migration path to S3-compatible object storage later = one URL change. Dolt v2.0.3 installed at /opt/homebrew/bin/dolt. First push completed 2026-05-17.

title: Infrastructure Foundation
description:
The foundational layer that collapses the local/cloud divide and provides the shared World State store. Everything in the system depends on this epic being complete first.

Components:
1. Dolt remote configuration for beads-global (DoltHub or self-hosted)
2. Deployed Beads Service (server/index.js as a persistent cloud service)
3. Supabase World State schema (calendar_snapshot, tasks, rules, recommendations, notification_log, user_preferences, patterns tables)
4. Context Collector v1 (Beads + Tasks adapters writing to World State on event trigger)

Why this first:
Without the Dolt remote, Beads data is invisible to cloud functions. Without the deployed Beads Service, no agent can read Beads issues. Without the World State schema, no agent has anywhere to write. Without Context Collector v1, the World State is empty. Every other epic reads from or writes to this foundation.

See ARD v2 §Implementation Sequence and §Beads-as-a-Service for full rationale.
close_reason: Closed

title: Build local API server bridging bd CLI to React app
description: The browser can't call bd CLI directly. A small Express server wraps bd --json commands and serves them over HTTP. Vite proxies /api/* to it during dev.
design: server/index.js: Express app with GET /api/beads/ready, /api/beads/list, /api/beads/show/:id. Each route runs bd with --json via child_process.execSync. Add proxy to vite.config.js. Add dev:full script to package.json running both servers concurrently.
close_reason: Express server in server/index.js wraps bd --json for /api/beads/ready|list|show/:id|stats. Vite proxies /api to port 3001. npm start runs both concurrently.

title: Restrict magic link sign-in to allowlisted users only
description:
## Problem
Supabase currently has sign-ups enabled. This means any email address can request a magic link and get a new auth.users row created. RLS protects data (unknown users see nothing), but the app is technically open to anyone who can receive an email.

## Fix
Two steps:
1. Disable sign-ups in Supabase: Dashboard → Authentication → Providers → Email → toggle off 'Enable sign-ups'. After this, magic links only work for emails already in auth.users — unknown emails are silently dropped.
2. Write a reusable SQL template (or bd formula) for adding new users by pre-inserting into auth.users, mirroring 20260526000001_create_auth_user_mo.sql.

## Adding users (post-fix)
New users must be manually pre-inserted via migration. No self-service registration.

## Why not urgent
RLS is the real protection — an unauthorized user who somehow got a link would land in an empty app. But disabling sign-ups is a 30-second fix that eliminates the ambiguity entirely.

title: Update auth-google-callback to store tokens under JWT user_id (not SUPABASE_USER_ID)
description:
After the bulk JWT replacement (replacing SUPABASE_USER_ID with extractUserId), auth-google-callback.js presents an additional challenge: the OAuth callback is a redirect from Google with no user session in the Authorization header. A mechanism is needed to associate the callback with the user who initiated the OAuth flow.

Fix: Update auth-google-start.js (from the CSRF fix issue) to encode the user's Supabase JWT into the state parameter alongside the CSRF token (e.g. state=csrfToken|userJwt or a signed payload). In auth-google-callback.js, after CSRF state verification, extract the JWT from the state parameter and call extractUserId with it to determine which user's preferences row to update.
design:
## Scenarios

- Given an authenticated user (Mo) clicks Connect Google → auth-google-start generates state containing CSRF token + Mo's JWT
  When Google redirect reaches auth-google-callback with matching state
  Then refresh token is stored in user_preferences for user_id = Mo's auth.uid()

- Given the state parameter carries a JWT that has since expired
  When auth-google-callback attempts extractUserId from state JWT
  Then returns 400 (cannot authenticate user), no token stored

- Given attacker constructs a callback with a valid CSRF state but swapped JWT (pointing to victim user)
  When callback verifies CSRF token from cookie
  Then state cookie mismatch → 400 before any token storage
close_reason: auth-google-start now embeds the caller's JWT as csrfToken:jwt in state; auth-google-callback splits on first colon, verifies CSRF from cookie, then resolves userId via supabase.auth.getUser(jwt). SUPABASE_USER_ID removed from both files. Committed in 25a139a.

title: Add missing RLS policies for category_weights, block_rejections, checkin_log
description:
Migration 20260524000002_c_new_tables.sql created category_weights, block_rejections, and checkin_log but lacks CREATE POLICY statements. Without RLS, these tables are readable/writable bypassing the per-user isolation enforced on all other tables.

Fix: New migration 20260526000000_add_rls_new_tables.sql that enables RLS and creates owner-only policies on all three tables.

## Scenarios

- Given an anon-key Supabase query to category_weights without auth
  When the query executes
  Then returns zero rows (RLS blocks unauthenticated access)

- Given an authenticated query with user A's JWT to category_weights
  When the query executes
  Then returns only rows where user_id = user A's auth.uid()

- Given the migration runs twice
  When db push runs again
  Then completes without error (DROP POLICY IF EXISTS guards idempotency), data unchanged

## Scenarios

- Given an anon-key Supabase query to category_weights without auth
  When the query executes
  Then returns zero rows (RLS blocks unauthenticated access)

- Given an authenticated query with user A's JWT to category_weights
  When the query executes
  Then returns only rows where user_id = user A's auth.uid()

- Given the migration runs twice (idempotent check via IF NOT EXISTS / DO NOTHING)
  When db push runs again
  Then completes without error, policies unchanged
close_reason: Migration 20260526000000_add_rls_new_tables.sql created: enables RLS and owner-only policies on category_weights, block_rejections, checkin_log. Idempotent via DROP POLICY IF EXISTS.

title: Add OAuth CSRF state parameter to Google auth flow
description:
netlify/functions/auth-google-callback.js processes the OAuth redirect without verifying a state parameter, enabling CSRF attacks where an attacker can attach their Google account to Mo's session.

Fix: Add netlify/functions/auth-google-start.js that generates a cryptographically random state token, stores it in Set-Cookie: oauth_state=<token>; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Max-Age=600 and returns the Google OAuth URL with state=<token>. Update auth-google-callback.js to compare req.query.state against the cookie value; reject 400 if missing or mismatched. Update the PWA connect callback to call auth-google-start first.

## Scenarios

- Given an attacker crafts a redirect to auth-google-callback with their Google code but no valid oauth_state cookie
  When the callback handler runs
  Then returns redirect to /settings?google=error&reason=csrf_mismatch, no token stored

- Given a legitimate auth flow: connect button calls auth-google-start, receives state cookie + URL, user completes consent, callback receives matching state
  When callback verifies state against cookie
  Then state matches, flow proceeds, refresh token stored, user redirected to /settings?google=connected

- Given the oauth_state cookie has expired (Max-Age=600)
  When callback attempts to verify state
  Then cookie is missing, redirect to error, user must restart the flow
design:
## Scenarios

- Given an attacker crafts a redirect to auth-google-callback with their own Google code but no valid oauth_state cookie
  When the callback handler runs
  Then returns 400, no Google token is stored

- Given a legitimate auth flow: user clicks Connect → auth-google-start sets cookie and returns URL → user completes Google consent → callback receives matching state
  When callback verifies state against cookie
  Then state matches, flow proceeds, refresh token stored correctly

- Given the oauth_state cookie has expired (Max-Age=600 exceeded)
  When the callback attempts to verify state
  Then returns 400, user must restart the auth flow
close_reason: auth-google-start.js generates random state token and sets HttpOnly/SameSite=Lax cookie. auth-google-callback.js verifies state vs cookie before processing code exchange; clears cookie on success. PWA connect() now calls server-side start function instead of building OAuth URL in browser.

title: Fix SSRF: reject private IP/hostname ranges in ical-feeds URL validation
description:
netlify/functions/ical-feeds.js stores arbitrary https:// URLs in user_preferences.ical_feeds. calendarSyncLogic.js later fetches them server-side, enabling SSRF attacks against internal network addresses, cloud metadata endpoints, and localhost.

Fix: In the ical-feeds.js POST validation block, after webcal:// normalization, extract the hostname via new URL() and reject: numeric IPv4 addresses, IPv6 literals, localhost, .local, .internal domains.

## Scenarios

- Given POST to /.netlify/functions/ical-feeds with { url: 'https://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/' }
  When URL validation runs
  Then returns 400 with error message, URL is NOT stored in ical_feeds

- Given POST with { url: 'https://192.168.1.100/ical/feed.ics' }
  When URL validation runs
  Then returns 400 (numeric IPv4 rejected), URL is NOT stored

- Given POST with a valid public iCal URL like https://outlook.office365.com/ical/...
  When URL validation runs
  Then passes validation, URL is stored, returns 200 with updated feeds array

- Given POST with { url: 'https://localhost/internal' }
  When URL validation runs
  Then returns 400 (localhost rejected)
close_reason: ical-feeds.js POST now extracts hostname via new URL() and rejects numeric IPv4, IPv6 literals, localhost, .local, .internal before storing. Simpler than range-based checks and covers all numeric IPs.

title: LifeOrganizer.jsx: replace flat RecommendationCard list with FocusCard + QueueCard portfolio UI
description:
Replace the current flat RecommendationCard list (renders claudeRecs.map(...)) with a
portfolio layout matching the new recommend.js response shape: { focus, queue, overdue, summary }.

Changes:
1. Update useClaudeRecommendations hook (inline in LifeOrganizer.jsx) to handle the new
   response shape. The hook currently returns a flat recommendations array; update to return
   { focus, queue, overdue, summary, ...rest }.

2. Create FocusCard component:
   - Prominent card (distinct styling from QueueCard)
   - Shows: title, reason, window (time slot)
   - Thumbs-up / thumbs-down buttons → call PATCH /recommendations
   - Empty state when focus is null: "Nothing fits your next block — mark a window as free or add a task"

3. Create QueueCard component:
   - Compact card, one per queue item
   - Shows: title, reason, window, source badge (beads/manual)
   - Thumbs-up / thumbs-down buttons → call PATCH /recommendations
   - Optimistic removal on thumbs-down (item disappears immediately without waiting for API)

4. Overdue items: DO NOT render in the recommendations section.
   Route them to the Alerts panel by merging with the notifications array.
   The overdue array items need to be shaped like notification objects:
   { title, body, source: "overdue" } (no rule_id, no notification_log id — cannot be dismissed
   to DB, only dismissed locally for the session).

5. Update the "Ask Claude" section:
   - Shows FocusCard if focus != null
   - Shows QueueCard list (0-3 items) in collapsible "Up Next" sub-section
   - Overdue items flow to Alerts panel automatically
   - Summary line displayed below FocusCard (existing summary rendering unchanged)

Dependencies: recommend.js portfolio upgrade (life-u34) must be deployed first,
PATCH /recommendations endpoint (life-cjv) must exist for thumbs to work.

## Scenarios

- Given recommend.js returns {focus:{...}, queue:[{...},{...}], overdue:[{...}], summary:"..."}
  When the PWA renders
  Then a prominent FocusCard shows the focus item with reason and time window
  And an "Up Next" section shows 2 QueueCards
  And the overdue item appears in the Alerts panel, not in the recommendation section

- Given focus is null (Claude returned focus: null)
  When the PWA renders
  Then the FocusCard area shows the empty state message
  And queue items still render in Up Next if present

- Given the user thumbs-down a QueueCard item
  When the action fires
  Then PATCH /recommendations is called with { historyId, ref, action:"dismissed" }
  And the card disappears immediately (optimistic update, no spinner)

- Given the API returns an overdue item AND a focus item
  When the PWA renders
  Then both are visible simultaneously — overdue in Alerts, focus in Focus Now
  (overdue items do not suppress or replace the focus card)

- Given recommend.js returns the old flat response shape (cached pre-migration response)
  When useClaudeRecommendations parses it
  Then it degrades gracefully — renders focus=null, queue=recommendations (mapped),
  overdue=[] rather than throwing
close_reason: Deployed to production. recommend.js now returns historyId (verified: 6). Portfolio UI live: FocusCard (indigo), QueueCard Up Next list, thumbs PATCH confirmed {ok:true}. Overdue items routed to Alerts with red styling and local dismiss. Backward compat preserved. All 5 scenarios pass.

title: recommend.js: portfolio prompt upgrade — focus/queue/overdue, category weights, free blocks, seed weights
description:
Upgrade recommend.js to produce a portfolio-shaped recommendation instead of a flat list.
This is the core Phase 1 intelligence change.

Changes:
1. Seed category_weights: on first run, if category_weights table is empty for this user,
   insert one row per category in user_preferences.categories at weight=0.5.

2. Read additional data from Supabase (parallel with existing reads):
   - user_preferences: weekly_schedule, schedule_exceptions, planning_window, categories
   - category_weights: latest weight per category (ORDER BY effective_from DESC, one per category)
   - block_rejections: active rows (released_at IS NULL) for this user

3. Compute structured availability using freeBlocks.js for each of the next 7 days.
   Format for Claude prompt:
     AVAILABLE TIME (next 7 days, America/Toronto):
       Today Wed May 28:  15:00-17:30 (150 min) ← preferred
                          19:00-20:00 (60 min)  ← evening
       Thu May 29:        09:00-11:30 (150 min), 14:00-18:00 (240 min) ← preferred
       Fri May 30:        free day (schedule exception)

4. Include category context per task and Beads issue in prompt.

5. Change Claude response shape to portfolio:
   { focus: {ref, reason, window} | null,
     queue: [{ref, reason, window}],
     overdue: [{ref, reason}],
     summary: string }

6. Resolve all three buckets (focus, queue, overdue) to full task objects using
   existing ref-resolution logic (extended from flatMap pattern already in file).

7. Store full portfolio in recommendation_history.content.
   Update cache invalidation to compare hash on portfolio hash (not just world state).

8. Add Beads priority guidance to Claude prompt:
   "Treat Beads priority as effort-sizing, not scheduling urgency.
    Use category weight + deadline to determine slot."

Dependencies: Migration A (user_preferences columns), Migration B (item_feedback),
Migration C (category_weights, block_rejections), freeBlocks.js utility.

## Scenarios

- Given category_weights is empty for the user
  When recommend.js runs
  Then it inserts 5 seed rows (one per default category at weight=0.5) before calling Claude
  And subsequent runs detect existing weights and do not re-seed

- Given working hours Mon-Fri 09:00-18:00, soft_ceiling 18:00, and a 2h free block at 10am today
  When recommend.js builds the Claude prompt
  Then AVAILABLE TIME shows structured blocks with preferred/evening tags (not raw event list)

- Given tasks with category:home (weight 0.7) and category:hobby (weight 0.3)
  When the prompt is built
  Then CATEGORY PRIORITIES includes home:0.7 and hobby:0.3
  And each task line includes its category label

- Given Claude returns valid portfolio JSON
  When recommend.js parses it
  Then the response body contains { focus, queue, overdue, summary, cached: false }
  And all refs are resolved to full task objects

- Given Claude returns an overdue ref that matches no open task (stale ref)
  When recommend.js processes the overdue array
  Then that ref is skipped with a console.warn (not a 500 error)

- Given the cache hash matches and is less than 30 minutes old
  When recommend.js runs
  Then it returns the cached portfolio without calling Claude

- Given focus is null (Claude returns focus: null — nothing fits next block)
  When the response is returned
  Then { focus: null, queue: [...], overdue: [...] } is valid and not an error
close_reason: recommend.js upgraded: portfolio shape (focus/queue/overdue), category_weights seeding, freeBlocks.js availability, category context in prompt, Beads priority guidance. 23/23 scenario checks passed. Commit fd6be97.

title: netlify/lib/freeBlocks.js: planning-window-aware free block computation utility
description:
Create netlify/lib/freeBlocks.js — a shared utility imported by both recommend.js and
evaluate-rules.js. Replaces the hardcoded 8pm EOD in evalOpenBlock and provides Claude
with structured availability instead of raw event lists.

The netlify/lib/ directory does not exist yet — create it.

Function signature:
  computeFreeBlocks(calendarEvents, weeklySchedule, scheduleExceptions,
                    planningWindow, rejectedBlocks, date, userTz)
  → [{ startISO, endISO, durationMinutes, preferred: bool }]

Algorithm:
  1. Check scheduleExceptions for this specific date
       type="off" → return [] (whole day free/off)
       type="custom" → use exception.hours as working window
  2. Find working window for date.dayOfWeek in weeklySchedule
       No entry → return [] (no working hours that day)
  3. Clip working window start against planningWindow.hard_floor
  4. Subtract sorted calendar events; gaps = free blocks
  5. Filter out active block_rejections (released_at IS NULL) overlapping this date
  6. Drop blocks shorter than DEFAULT_MIN_BLOCK_MINUTES (20)
  7. Tag each block: preferred = block.start < planningWindow.soft_ceiling
  8. Return sorted array by startISO

All time comparisons done in userTz using date-fns-tz or native Intl (match pattern
used elsewhere in the codebase — check recommend.js and ical-sync.js for precedent).

Dependencies: requires Migration A (weekly_schedule, schedule_exceptions, planning_window
columns) to exist in user_preferences before callers can pass real data.

## Scenarios

- Given working hours 09:00-18:00, calendar events 10:00-11:30 and 14:00-15:00, no rejections
  When computeFreeBlocks is called for that date
  Then returns three blocks: 09:00-10:00 (60min preferred), 11:30-14:00 (150min preferred),
  15:00-18:00 (180min preferred)

- Given planning_window hard_floor="09:00" and working window starts at 08:00
  When computeFreeBlocks runs
  Then no block starts before 09:00 (hard_floor clips the window start)

- Given soft_ceiling="18:00" and a 2-hour gap 19:00-21:00 within working hours
  When computeFreeBlocks runs
  Then the 19:00-21:00 block is returned with preferred=false (evening block)

- Given schedule_exceptions contains {date:"2026-05-30", type:"off"}
  When computeFreeBlocks is called for 2026-05-30
  Then returns [] immediately regardless of weekly_schedule or events

- Given an active block_rejection (released_at=null) for day_of_week matching the date, 14:00-16:00
  When computeFreeBlocks runs for that day
  Then 14:00-16:00 is excluded from results even if schedule shows it free

- Given a released block_rejection (released_at set) for the same slot
  When computeFreeBlocks runs
  Then the 14:00-16:00 window IS included (released rejections are ignored)

- Given all events fill the day with no gap >= 20 minutes
  When computeFreeBlocks runs
  Then returns []
close_reason: netlify/lib/freeBlocks.js implemented and pushed. 16/16 scenario checks passed locally: basic gap computation, hard_floor clipping, soft_ceiling preferred flag, off-day exception, active/released block rejections, and all-busy day. Commit 159e167.

title: Migration C: create category_weights, block_rejections, checkin_log tables
description:
Three new tables required for the intelligence feedback loop.

category_weights: user-defined and Pattern Analyzer-learned priorities per category.
No CHECK constraint on category column — dynamic categories. Seeded by recommend.js on
first run (5 rows at weight=0.5). Includes set_by field to distinguish user vs system.

block_rejections: stores "keep this block free" signals with reversibility. released_at
timestamptz is null for active rejections, set to a timestamp when user releases the block.
freeBlocks.js filters WHERE released_at IS NULL.

checkin_log: stores periodic collaborative questions/answers for Phase 3 check-ins.
Created now so the schema is stable; no code writes to it until Phase 3.

SQL to apply:
  create table public.category_weights (
    id             bigint generated by default as identity primary key,
    user_id        uuid not null references auth.users on delete cascade,
    category       text not null,
    weight         real not null default 0.5,
    rationale      text,
    set_by         text not null default 'user',
    effective_from timestamptz not null default now(),
    expires_at     timestamptz,
    created_at     timestamptz not null default now()
  );
  create index on public.category_weights (user_id, category, effective_from desc);

  create table public.block_rejections (
    id           bigint generated by default as identity primary key,
    user_id      uuid not null references auth.users on delete cascade,
    rejected_at  timestamptz not null default now(),
    day_of_week  smallint not null,
    start_time   time not null,
    end_time     time not null,
    source       text not null default 'user',
    released_at  timestamptz
  );

  create table public.checkin_log (
    id            bigint generated by default as identity primary key,
    user_id       uuid not null references auth.users on delete cascade,
    trigger_type  text not null,
    trigger_event text,
    questions     jsonb not null,
    answers       jsonb,
    model         text,
    generated_at  timestamptz not null default now(),
    answered_at   timestamptz,
    created_at    timestamptz not null default now()
  );

Write migration file to supabase/migrations/20260524_c_new_tables.sql.

## Scenarios

- Given the migration runs on a fresh Supabase project
  When all three CREATE TABLE statements execute
  Then category_weights, block_rejections, and checkin_log all exist with correct columns

- Given recommend.js seeds category_weights with 5 rows at weight=0.5
  When those rows are inserted
  Then querying category_weights for the user returns exactly 5 rows, one per default category

- Given a block_rejection row with released_at=null is inserted
  When queried with WHERE released_at IS NULL
  Then the row is returned (active rejection)

- Given that same row has released_at set to a timestamp
  When queried with WHERE released_at IS NULL
  Then the row is NOT returned (released — no longer an active rejection)

- Given the index on category_weights(user_id, category, effective_from desc) exists
  When recommend.js queries for the latest weight per category
  Then the query uses the index (ORDER BY effective_from DESC LIMIT 1 per category)
close_reason: All three migrations applied via supabase db push. Verified: user_preferences has weekly_schedule/schedule_exceptions/planning_window/categories; category_weights and block_rejections tables exist; open_tasks category default migrated to adhoc.

title: Migration B: update open_tasks category default, add beads_ready.labels, recommendation_history.item_feedback
description:
Three related column changes to complete the data model for Phase 1:

1. open_tasks.category: migrate existing rows with category='general' to 'adhoc';

2. beads_ready.labels: add JSONB column for future Beads-to-category mapping.

  update public.open_tasks set category = 'adhoc' where category = 'general';

  alter table public.beads_ready add column if not exists labels jsonb not null default '[]';

title: Migration A: add weekly_schedule, schedule_exceptions, planning_window, categories to user_preferences
description:
The intelligence layer requires per-user schedule data before freeBlocks.js can operate.
Four new JSONB columns on user_preferences provide the data model foundation:
weekly_schedule (working hours by day-of-week), schedule_exceptions (date-specific overrides),
planning_window (hard floor + soft ceiling constraints), and categories (user-configurable
list replacing the hard-coded taxonomy).

This is the first of three Phase 1 migrations and is a hard dependency for freeBlocks.js,
recommend.js, and evaluate-rules.js. Must be applied before any other Phase 1 code lands.

SQL to apply (Supabase dashboard → SQL editor):

  alter table public.user_preferences
    add column if not exists weekly_schedule jsonb not null default '[
      {"day":1,"start":"09:00","end":"18:00"},
      {"day":2,"start":"09:00","end":"18:00"},
      {"day":3,"start":"09:00","end":"18:00"},
      {"day":4,"start":"09:00","end":"18:00"},
      {"day":5,"start":"09:00","end":"18:00"}
    ]',
    add column if not exists schedule_exceptions jsonb not null default '[]',
    add column if not exists planning_window jsonb not null default '{"hard_floor":"06:00","soft_ceiling":"18:00"}',
    add column if not exists categories jsonb not null default '["professional","home","hobby","social","adhoc"]';

Also write the migration file to supabase/migrations/20260524_a_prefs_columns.sql.

## Scenarios

- Given user_preferences has existing rows with no value for the new columns
  When the migration is applied
  Then all four columns exist with defaults: weekly_schedule=Mon-Fri 9-18,
  schedule_exceptions=[], planning_window={hard_floor:"06:00",soft_ceiling:"18:00"},
  categories=["professional","home","hobby","social","adhoc"]
  And existing rows are backfilled with those defaults automatically

- Given the migration file is applied twice (idempotency check)
  When the second apply runs
  Then no error is thrown (add column if not exists guards each column)

- Given recommend.js reads weekly_schedule for a user who accepted all defaults
  When the SELECT runs
  Then it receives a 5-entry array covering Mon–Fri

- Given a new user_preferences row is inserted with no explicit values for the new columns
  When the INSERT completes
  Then all four columns have their default values without the caller specifying them

title: Pass user timezone to Claude for time-aware recommendations
description:
recommend.js constructs the Claude prompt using new Date() on the Netlify server (UTC). Claude is told the wrong current time and has no idea what timezone the calendar events are in. This causes bad reasoning about available time blocks.

user_preferences already has a timezone column (text, default 'America/Toronto') but it is never read by recommend.js.

What needs to change:
- recommend.js: read user_preferences.timezone at the start alongside other world state reads
- Replace the UTC-based dateStr/timeStr in the prompt with the equivalent formatted in the user's timezone using Intl.DateTimeFormat
- Add the user's timezone to the calendar section header so Claude knows how to interpret event times
- Fix the calendar_snapshot query: use the user's local date (not UTC) as the lower bound

## Scenarios

- Given user timezone is America/New_York and it is 2026-05-23T22:00:00Z (6 PM ET)
  When recommend.js builds the Claude prompt
  Then the prompt says the current time is Friday May 23 at 06:00 PM not Saturday May 24 (UTC)

- Given calendar events on May 23 stored with times like 14:30-15:30
  When the prompt is built
  Then the calendar section includes a header indicating times are in America/New_York

- Given the user's local date is May 23 but the UTC date is May 24
  When the calendar_snapshot query runs
  Then it fetches rows for May 23 onward (user's local date) not May 24 (UTC date)
close_reason: All three deployed to https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app. node-ical handles TZID correctly; recommend.js reads user_preferences.timezone for prompt, calendar query, and event formatting; events deduplicated by id before Claude sees them.

title: Replace inline iCal parser with node-ical for correct TZID handling
description:
The inline parser in ical-sync.js strips timezone info from TZID events (e.g. DTSTART;TZID=America/New_York:20260523T100000) and emits a bare ISO string with no offset. This means iCal events with TZID are stored in calendar_snapshot with wrong times — off by the user's UTC offset. node-ical correctly converts TZID to UTC Date objects.

What needs to change:
- npm install node-ical
- Replace parseICS / parseDateVal / normalizeEvents in ical-sync.js with node-ical's ical.async.fromICS(icsText)
- All events (Google and Apple) should store start/end as full ISO UTC strings in calendar_snapshot so recommend.js has consistent, unambiguous data

## Scenarios

- Given an iCal event DTSTART;TZID=America/New_York:20260523T100000
  When ical-sync fetches and parses the feed
  Then the event is stored in calendar_snapshot with start=2026-05-23T14:00:00.000Z (correct UTC) not 2026-05-23T10:00:00 (wrong)

- Given an iCal event DTSTART:20260523T180000Z (already UTC)
  When ical-sync parses it
  Then start is stored as 2026-05-23T18:00:00.000Z unchanged

- Given an all-day event DTSTART;VALUE=DATE:20260523
  When ical-sync parses it
  Then startISO is null and the event is classified as type='event'
close_reason: All three deployed to https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app. node-ical handles TZID correctly; recommend.js reads user_preferences.timezone for prompt, calendar query, and event formatting; events deduplicated by id before Claude sees them.

title: Google OAuth infrastructure: server-side auth code flow for Calendar + Gmail
description:
Both Calendar (life-5eu) and Gmail (life-9br) require server-side OAuth with refresh tokens stored in Supabase. Building them on the same infrastructure is cheaper than two separate flows. This issue provisions that shared foundation.

Scope:
- Single Google Cloud project, single OAuth 2.0 client (Web App type)
- Consent screen scopes: calendar.readonly + gmail.readonly
- One Netlify Function: GET /api/auth/google/callback — exchanges auth code for access + refresh tokens
- One token refresh helper (called by Calendar adapter and Gmail cron before each API call)
- Refresh tokens stored in Supabase user_preferences (per ARD secrets inventory)

Setup steps:
1. Google Cloud Console: enable Calendar API + Gmail API on the same project
2. OAuth consent screen: add both scopes, mark Internal (personal use — no Google review)
3. Create OAuth 2.0 credentials (Web App): add redirect URIs localhost:5173/api/auth/google/callback + Netlify domain equivalent
4. Add GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID (Netlify + .env.local) and GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET (Netlify only)
5. Implement GET /api/auth/google/callback Netlify Function: exchange code, store refresh tokens in Supabase user_preferences
6. Implement POST /api/auth/google/refresh helper: takes grant type + stored refresh token, returns fresh access token
7. Add Connect Google button in PWA settings panel that initiates the auth code flow

Note: GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET is already in the ARD secrets inventory as a Netlify-only secret — this was anticipated from the start.

## Scenarios

- Given a logged-in user clicks 'Connect Google' in the PWA settings panel
  When the OAuth consent flow completes and Google redirects to /api/auth/google/callback with a valid auth code
  Then the callback function exchanges the code for access + refresh tokens, stores the refresh token in user_preferences.google_refresh_token in Supabase, and redirects the PWA to /settings?google=connected

- Given a refresh token stored in user_preferences for a user
  When the /api/auth/google/refresh Netlify function is POSTed with the user's session token
  Then it reads the stored refresh token, calls Google's token endpoint, and returns a fresh access token (valid for 1 hour) — without exposing the refresh token to the browser

- Given the Connect Google button has been clicked and the user completes consent
  When the callback receives an auth code that Google has already invalidated (e.g. code reuse or expired)
  Then the callback returns a 400 error JSON response and the PWA shows 'Google connection failed — please try again' messaging

- Given a user_preferences row that already has a google_refresh_token set
  When the user visits /settings
  Then the Connect Google button shows as 'Google Connected' (disabled/success state) rather than prompting re-auth
notes:
E2E verified 2026-05-22. Three bugs discovered and fixed post-initial-close:
1. GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET/ID not actually saved to Netlify (MCP call silently failed) — fixed by setting via CLI.
2. Netlify env:set --scope + --context flags required specific ordering; vars now set in 'all' context.
3. auth-google-callback used UPDATE which is a silent no-op when user_preferences row absent — changed to UPSERT. Seeded user_preferences row via REST API.
Final state: Settings shows 'Google Connected' badge on cold reload (server-side auth-google-status returns connected:true from Supabase). google_refresh_token column SET (103 chars) for user 37b3da7e. Commits: 5c0195a feat, e0f902b close, 8a621d7 SW fix, 69c0db3 upsert fix. All pushed to origin/main.
close_reason: Migration applied. Code committed + pushed. GCP OAuth client created (life-organizer-497121), Calendar + Gmail APIs enabled. Redirect URIs set for localhost:8888 + life-organizer-mo.netlify.app. Netlify env vars GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID all set. Test user g.mofro@gmail.com added to OAuth consent screen. Ready for end-to-end test.

title: Decide Calendar OAuth flow: client-side session vs. server-side refresh token
description:
life-5eu currently plans a client-side Google Identity Services (GIS) implicit token flow — access token stored in sessionStorage, expires in 1 hour, only available when PWA is open.

This is correct IF calendar data will always be user-session-only (Context Collector only runs when the user opens the app).

It is WRONG IF calendar data is needed for background work (e.g. the hourly Rules Engine cron sweep, or a server-side Context Collector trigger).

The ARD's secrets inventory lists 'Calendar OAuth refresh token → Supabase per-user row' — implying server-side flow was intended.

Options:
  A. Client-side only (GIS implicit): simpler, no Netlify Function needed for auth, token dies when tab closes. Rules Engine cron would have no calendar data outside user sessions.
  B. Server-side auth code flow: one-time consent in browser, refresh token stored in Supabase, calendar data available to all server functions. More setup, but consistent with how Gmail OAuth is designed.

Impact: decision affects life-5eu architecture, the hourly Rules Engine cron's access to calendar data, and whether the Context Collector can run server-side.
notes: CRITICAL CONTEXT MISSING FROM THIS ISSUE: The hourly Rules Engine cron (evaluate-rules.js) reads calendar_snapshot FROM SUPABASE — it never calls Google Calendar API directly. The cron does not need a Google token. The only component that needs a Google token is the Calendar adapter (life-5eu), which writes to calendar_snapshot. So the real question is: how fresh must calendar_snapshot be, and is user-session-only refresh acceptable? ARD event bus lists Context Collector as triggered by 'App opened' and 'manual refresh' — background calendar collection is NOT in the v1 design. IMPLICATION: Option A (client-side GIS implicit) is likely correct for v1. Server-side (Option B) is only needed if you want calendar_snapshot refreshed in the background while the PWA is closed. Decide: is daily staleness acceptable (user opens app once a day → snapshots are <24h old) or do you want fresher data?
close_reason: Decision: server-side auth code flow. life-8li now covers the full Google OAuth infrastructure (Calendar + Gmail) as a shared foundation. life-5eu depends on life-8li.

title: Context Reasoner: on-demand Claude recommendations from World State
description:
On-demand Claude call that takes full World State and returns rich ranked recommendations with explanations. Replaces the current rules-based scoring algorithm in LifeOrganizer.jsx.

This is the 'What should I work on right now and why?' button.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Where Claude Belongs):
Claude is given: calendar today (free blocks, committed time), open tasks (all sources, priority/deadline), Beads issues ready, recent user actions (what was accepted/dismissed). Returns: ranked list with specific reasons tied to current context, not generic priority scores.

Example output:
  1. life-b45 (Rules Engine v1) — your 2pm meeting just ended, you have 90 min free, this is a 1-2h task, and it unblocks 3 other issues
  2. Reply to Sarah's email — implicit deadline (she needs an answer today), 15 min
  3. life-57m (World State schema) — P0 blocker, nothing else can proceed without it

Implementation:
1. Create Netlify function: POST /api/recommend with worldState payload.
2. Call Anthropic API: structured prompt with world state sections, request ranked JSON response.
3. Cache response in recommendation_history (invalidate on open_tasks or beads_ready change, not on notification_log writes).
4. Add 'Ask Claude' button to PWA recommendation panel.
5. Replace getRecommendations() local scoring algorithm with Claude output.
6. Store user_action (accepted/dismissed) against each recommendation for Pattern Analyzer.

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1 — need rule firing history for context)
Part of: EPIC-INTEL (life-39b)

## Scenarios

- Given the user has open tasks and ready Beads issues in World State
  When the user clicks 'Ask Claude'
  Then the Recommendation panel shows Claude's ranked list with contextual reasons tied to current time and calendar, and the result is stored in recommendation_history

- Given a recommendation was returned less than 30 minutes ago and World State has not changed
  When the user clicks 'Ask Claude' again
  Then the cached result is returned instantly without a new Anthropic API call (recommendation_history created_at confirms no new row)

- Given the user has no open tasks and no ready Beads issues
  When the user clicks 'Ask Claude'
  Then Claude returns a sensible empty-state message rather than an error, and the UI displays it gracefully

- Given the Anthropic API call fails (network error, rate limit, or timeout)
  When the user clicks 'Ask Claude'
  Then the UI shows a clear error state, the previous local scoring results remain visible, and no partial recommendation_history row is written
notes: TABLE EXISTS: the Supabase table is recommendation_history (not 'recommendations'). Columns: id, user_id, content (jsonb array of recommendations), world_state_snapshot (jsonb), user_action, model, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, created_at, updated_at. SCORING ALGORITHM STILL PRESENT: getRecommendations() function still exists in LifeOrganizer.jsx (lines 15-49) along with MOCK_CALENDAR. This is the exact code this issue replaces — confirmed, it is not already removed. CACHE INVALIDATION: define trigger precisely before implementing. Suggestion: invalidate when open_tasks or beads_ready changes (not on every notification_log write, which happens on every rule evaluation).
close_reason: Context Reasoner deployed and verified end-to-end. recommend.js calls claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 with world state from Supabase, returns 3 ranked recs with reasons, 30-min cache on world state hash. Verified: correct recs returned in production (overdue task, due-today task, P1 Beads issue). Local scoring function getRecommendations() removed. UI shows Ask Claude button with loading/cached states.

title: Rules Engine v1: 4 seed rules evaluating World State
description:
Implement the deterministic Rules Engine that evaluates a seed set of rules against World State on every state change. No AI involved — pure condition evaluation.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Rules Engine Pattern):
Rules are stored in Supabase rules table. Engine reads enabled rules, evaluates each condition against current World State, checks cooldown via notification_log, fires matches to Notification Dispatcher.

4 seed rules to implement:
  open-block-match: free_block.duration >= task.time_required AND task.priority IN (high, critical) AND task.status = pending → 'You have {duration} free — {task} fits'
  deadline-proximity: task.deadline - now <= 24h AND task.status != completed → 'Deadline tomorrow: {task}'
  beads-unblocked: beads_issue.all_blocked_by_closed = true AND priority <= P1 → '{issue} is now unblocked'
  stalled-deadline: days_since_progress >= 3 AND deadline - now <= 7 days → '{task} has stalled — {N} days left'

Implementation:
1. Create evaluateRules(worldState, rules) pure function.
2. Check cooldowns against notification_log.
3. Write matches to notification_log (fired_at, rule_id, context_payload).
4. Expose rule match events for Notification Dispatcher to consume.
5. Run on: app open, manual refresh, and Supabase realtime subscription to tasks/calendar_snapshot tables.

Depends on: life-4qr (Context Collector v1 — needs World State data to evaluate)
Part of: EPIC-INTEL (life-39b)

## Scenarios

- Given open_tasks has a task with deadline within 24h and status != completed
  When the Rules Engine evaluates World State
  Then deadline_proximity fires, a row is written to notification_log with the rule_id and task context, and the match is returned to the caller

- Given a rule fired less than cooldown_minutes ago (recorded in notification_log)
  When the Rules Engine evaluates the same rule against the same World State
  Then the rule is skipped and no duplicate notification_log row is written

- Given open_tasks is empty and beads_ready has no unblocked P0/P1 issues
  When the Rules Engine runs
  Then no rules fire, notification_log is unchanged, and the engine returns an empty matches array without errors

- Given a Beads issue in beads_ready has all its blocked_by entries resolved (empty array) and priority <= P1
  When the Rules Engine evaluates World State
  Then the issue_unblocked rule fires and a notification_log row is written with the issue context

NOTES
Code deployed to Netlify (netlify/functions/evaluate-rules.js) with 4 seed rules: deadline_proximity, issue_unblocked, task_stalled, open_block. NOT yet tested — open_tasks table in Supabase is empty. Next session: (1) claim, (2) write scenarios, (3) seed test tasks in Supabase, (4) verify each rule fires correctly, (5) close with evidence.
notes: Code deployed to Netlify (netlify/functions/evaluate-rules.js) with 4 seed rules: deadline_proximity, issue_unblocked, task_stalled, open_block. NOT yet tested — open_tasks table in Supabase is empty. Next session: (1) claim, (2) write scenarios, (3) seed test tasks in Supabase, (4) verify each rule fires correctly, (5) close with evidence.
close_reason: Rules Engine verified in production. deadline_proximity fires for task with 12h deadline; cooldown correctly suppresses re-fire 5s later. notification_log insert confirmed working with channel/title/body columns. issue_unblocked/task_stalled/open_block no-op correctly with no matching data.

title: Intelligence Layer
description:
The deterministic Rules Engine and the two AI-powered reasoning agents. This is where the system earns the word 'intelligent'.

Components:
1. Rules Engine v1: evaluate 4 seed rules against World State on every state change. Deterministic, no AI, near-zero cost.
2. Context Reasoner: on-demand Claude call with full World State → ranked recommendations with explanations. User-triggered.
3. Pattern Analyzer: daily/weekly Claude call reviewing rule firing history → suggest rule adjustments. One call per cycle.

Rule evaluation is fast, cheap, and always-on. Claude is invoked at exactly two points: when the user asks for advice (Context Reasoner), and when the system reviews its own behaviour (Pattern Analyzer).

Depends on: EPIC-INFRA (Rules Engine needs World State to evaluate against)

See ARD v2 §Rules Engine Pattern and §Where Claude Belongs.

title: Calendar Context Collector — Google Calendar adapter feeding World State
description:
Personal Google Calendar is the first structured source to plug into the Context Collector layer. This makes calendar data available to the Rules Engine for free-block matching and schedule-aware recommendations.

Architectural context (ARD v2):
This is a Context Collector adapter, not a standalone feature. Its output goes into the World State (Supabase calendar_snapshot), where the Rules Engine can evaluate open-block-match and schedule-aware rules against it.

Implementation plan:
1. Google Cloud Console: create OAuth 2.0 credentials (Web App). Scopes: calendar.readonly. Authorised origins: localhost:5173 + production URL.
2. Add VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID to .env.local and Netlify env vars.
3. Load Google Identity Services in index.html.
4. Create useGoogleCalendar hook: initTokenClient, requestAccessToken on mount, store token in sessionStorage.
5. Fetch events: GET /calendar/v3/calendars/primary/events (timeMin=now, timeMax=+7d, singleEvents=true).
6. Normalise to WorldState calendar_snapshot shape: {id, title, start, end, duration_minutes, type, free_block_before, free_block_after}.
7. Write snapshot to Supabase calendar_snapshot table (upsert on event id).
8. Handle token expiry: re-request on 401.
9. Depends on: EPIC-INFRA (World State schema must exist before this can write to it).

## Scenarios

  When [describe trigger or action]
  Then [describe expected outcome]

title: Beads write-back: claim and close from UI via local API
description:
Beads tasks are currently read-only in the UI. The accordion shows 'bd close <id>' as a text hint but cannot execute it. Since the local Express server is already running when the app is used locally, claim and close should be possible directly from the UI for that workflow.

Implementation Plan:

2. Add POST /api/beads/close/:id to server — accepts JSON body {reason}, runs: bd close <id> --reason='<reason>'. Returns {ok, error}.

4. Clicking Close reveals an inline reason input (text field + confirm/cancel) before POSTing — no modal.
5. Both buttons show tooltip 'server offline' and are disabled when /api/health check fails.

title: Add security headers block to netlify.toml
description:
netlify.toml has no [[headers]] configuration. Without security headers, browsers have no framing protection (clickjacking), MIME sniffing protection, or CSP restricting script/connect origins.

Fix: Add a [[headers]] block to netlify.toml with X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin, Permissions-Policy restricting camera/microphone/geolocation, and a CSP allowing self + Supabase + Anthropic origins. Note: CSP must include 'unsafe-inline' for script-src due to current Vite/Tailwind setup.

## Scenarios

- Given any HTTP request to life-organizer-mo.netlify.app/*
  When response headers are inspected (curl -I)
  Then X-Frame-Options: DENY is present

- Given any HTTP request
  When response headers are inspected
  Then X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff and Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin are present

- Given a page that attempts to embed the app in an iframe
  When the browser enforces X-Frame-Options
  Then the embed is blocked

- Given a page that attempts to load a script from an unauthorized CDN
  When the browser enforces CSP
  Then the script is blocked (connect-src and script-src only allow self + configured origins)
