title: Sanitize error responses in beads-show and beads-create
description:
netlify/functions/beads-show.js and netlify/functions/beads-create.js forward raw Railway error responses to the client. Railway error bodies may include internal filesystem paths, stack traces, or other implementation details.

Fix: Catch non-2xx responses from Railway, log the full error body server-side, return sanitized { error: 'upstream error' } with appropriate HTTP status.

## Scenarios

- Given Railway returns HTTP 500 with body containing an internal path
  When beads-show forwards the response
  Then client receives { error: 'upstream error' } with status 500, internal details not exposed

- Given Railway is unreachable (network timeout)
  When beads-show attempts the fetch
  Then client receives { error: 'upstream error' } with status 503

- Given Railway returns a valid 200 response
  When beads-show forwards it
  Then client receives the full response body unchanged
design:
## Scenarios

- Given Railway returns HTTP 500 with body {"error": "ENOENT: /app/.beads/issues.jsonl not found"}
  When beads-show forwards the response
  Then client receives {"error": "upstream error"} with status 500, path not exposed in response

- Given Railway is unreachable (network timeout)
  When beads-show attempts the fetch
  Then client receives {"error": "upstream error"} with status 503

- Given Railway returns a valid 200 response
  When beads-show forwards the response
  Then client receives the full response body unchanged (sanitization only applies to errors)
close_reason: beads-show.js and beads-create.js now log full Railway errors server-side and return sanitized { error: 'upstream error' } to client.

title: Multi-feed iCal: replace single apple_ical_url with ical_feeds array
description: Replace the single apple_ical_url text column with an ical_feeds jsonb array so users can connect multiple iCal sources (iCloud, Outlook, etc.) simultaneously. Label each feed from its URL domain. Remove the hardcoded Apple label.
close_reason: Deployed. ical_feeds jsonb column live in Supabase. ical-feeds.js GET/POST/DELETE tested — add/remove works, sync fetches both feeds in parallel. UI shows per-feed labels (iCloud/Outlook/etc) with individual remove buttons. Duplicate migration file removed.

title: Feedback loop: inject past accept/dismiss signals into Claude prompt
description:
## Why
The `item_feedback` column in `recommendation_history` accumulates every thumbs-up/down the user gives, but the recommendation engine never reads it. Claude repeatedly recommends items the user has already dismissed, wasting tokens and degrading trust. Closing this loop makes recommendations progressively smarter.

## What
In `netlify/functions/recommend.js` POST path only:

1. After loading world state, add a parallel DB query for the last 10 `recommendation_history` rows with non-empty `item_feedback`.
2. Flatten and aggregate per ref: `{ 'task:123': { dismissed: 2, accepted: 1 }, 'beads:life-abc': { dismissed: 3 } }`.
3. Build a PAST FEEDBACK prompt section (omit if empty):
```
PAST FEEDBACK (last 10 sessions):
  Frequently dismissed — only recommend if deadline is imminent or conditions changed:
    - beads:life-abc "Add search" dismissed:3x
    - task:123 "Clean garage" dismissed:2x
  Accepted — user engaged with these:
    - task:456 "Write quarterly report" accepted:2x
```
4. Inject before the Rules section in the prompt.
5. Add to Rules: "Items dismissed 2+ times recently: only include in focus/queue if deadline is OVERDUE or < 2 days away."
6. To resolve titles for refs in feedback, cross-reference against the already-loaded `openTasks` and `beadsReady` arrays.

## Scenarios

- Given an item has been dismissed 2+ times across recent history
  When a fresh POST /recommend fires
  Then the Claude prompt contains a PAST FEEDBACK section naming that item as frequently dismissed

- Given no `item_feedback` exists in any recent history rows
  When POST /recommend fires
  Then no PAST FEEDBACK section is injected into the prompt

- Given Claude receives the PAST FEEDBACK section with a frequently-dismissed item
  When the response is parsed
  Then that item does not appear in focus or queue unless its deadline is OVERDUE or < 2 days away

- Given feedback history contains both dismissed and accepted refs
  When POST /recommend fires
  Then both sections (frequently dismissed + accepted) appear in the PAST FEEDBACK block
close_reason: buildFeedbackText() added to recommend.js POST path. Aggregates last 10 sessions of item_feedback, injects PAST FEEDBACK section into Claude prompt with suppression rule for dismissed items.

title: PWA: auto-restore recommendations on mount + lift dismissed state + fix feedback button UX
description:
## Why
After the GET /recommend endpoint exists (life-NNN), the PWA hook needs to call it on mount so recommendations appear immediately on page load. Additionally, dismissed card state is currently local React state — it resets on reload and is lost. The fix is to derive the dismissed set from `item_feedback` returned by the GET endpoint. Finally, the 👍/👎 buttons are tiny (~20px touch targets) and awkwardly placed on mobile; they need proper 44px tap targets moved to a bottom action row.

## What

### Hook (`useClaudeRecommendations` in LifeOrganizer.jsx)
- Add `dismissed` (`Set<string>`) state and `stale` (bool) state.
- Add mount effect: call GET /recommend, populate all state, initialize `dismissed` from returned `item_feedback` (collect refs with `action: 'dismissed'`).
- Add `dismiss(ref)` function: adds ref to dismissed Set + sends PATCH feedback fire-and-forget.
- Export `dismissed`, `dismiss`, `stale` from hook.

### FocusCard / QueueCard
- Remove local `useState(false)` for dismissed.
- Accept `dismissed: Set`, `onDismiss: fn` props.
- Compute ref at render time; hide card when `dismissed.has(ref)`.
- Move 👍/👎 to a bottom action row with `min-h-[44px]` tap targets and text labels ("Good" / "Skip").

### Main rendering
- Pass `dismissed` and `dismiss` to each card.
- Show "from N hours ago" stale indicator when `recoStale && hasReco`.

## Scenarios

- Given the last recommendation is < 30 min old
  When the PWA loads
  Then the Focus Now card and Up Next queue appear immediately without any button press

- Given a card was dismissed in a previous session (action stored in `item_feedback`)
  When the PWA reloads
  Then that card does not appear (dismissed Set is restored from DB)

- Given recommendations are stale (> 30 min old)
  When the PWA loads
  Then a subtle timestamp ("from N hours ago") is visible near the Refresh button

- Given a user taps the Skip/dismiss button on a recommendation card
  When the interaction fires
  Then the card disappears, PATCH feedback is sent, and the tap target is at least 44px tall (comfortable on mobile)
close_reason: useClaudeRecommendations hook updated: GET on mount, lifted dismissed Set from item_feedback, dismiss/accept callbacks. FocusCard/QueueCard updated with 44px bottom action row. Stale timestamp rendered in controls.

title: GET /recommend — restore last recommendation without calling Claude
description:
## Why
The PWA currently loses all recommendation state on every page reload. The backend already persists every recommendation in `recommendation_history` (Supabase), but there is no GET path on the endpoint — only POST triggers a new Claude call. We need a cheap read-only path that restores the last recommendation so sessions can pick up where they left off.

## What
Add GET method handling to `netlify/functions/recommend.js`:
- GET → query `recommendation_history` for the most recent row for this user; return its `content` (focus/queue/overdue/summary), `historyId`, `item_feedback`, and a `stale: bool` flag (stale = age > 30 min).
- POST → unchanged (existing generation + cache logic).
- If no history row exists → return `{ emptyState: true }`.
- Add `restored: true` to GET responses so the PWA can distinguish them from fresh POST responses.

## Scenarios

- Given a `recommendation_history` row exists that is < 30 min old
  When GET /.netlify/functions/recommend is called
  Then it returns `content`, `historyId`, `item_feedback`, `stale: false`, `restored: true` without calling the Anthropic API

- Given a `recommendation_history` row exists that is > 30 min old
  When GET /.netlify/functions/recommend is called
  Then it returns the same fields with `stale: true` (no Claude call)

- Given no `recommendation_history` rows exist for the user
  When GET /.netlify/functions/recommend is called
  Then it returns `{ emptyState: true }` with HTTP 200

- Given a POST request is made
  When the handler runs
  Then the existing generation + cache logic executes unchanged (GET path is not triggered)
close_reason: GET /recommend implemented in netlify/functions/recommend.js. Returns last history row with stale flag and item_feedback, no Claude call.

title: LifeOrganizer.jsx: persist notification dismissals + reload undismissed alerts on app open
description:
Two related changes to close the alert lifecycle loop. Currently dismiss() removes an alert
from local state only; reopening the app resets all alerts to empty until evaluate runs.

Change 1: dismiss write-back
  In useRulesEngine.js, when dismiss(index) is called:
    - Get the notification_log id from the notification object (requires evaluate-rules.js
      to return id in the fired array — see life-199)
    - Fire PATCH /notifications?id=<id> with body {action:"dismissed"} (fire-and-forget)
    - Remove from local state immediately (no blocking on API response)
    - If API call fails, log to console only — local dismiss still happens

Change 2: notification persistence on app open
  In useRulesEngine.js, add a loadPersistedNotifications() function:
    - Calls GET /notifications?channel=in_app&since=<24h ago>&action=null
      (this GET endpoint is added to notifications.js per life-cjv)
    - Returns notification_log rows: { id, rule_id, title, body, payload, fired_at }
    - Merges these into the notifications state array on mount
    - De-duplicates by id if evaluate() also fires the same rule in the same session
  Call loadPersistedNotifications() on component mount (alongside or before evaluate()).

useRulesEngine.js is currently at src/useRulesEngine.js.

Dependencies:
  - evaluate-rules.js must return notification_log.id in fired array (life-199)
  - PATCH /notifications endpoint must exist (life-cjv — also adds GET variant)

## Scenarios

- Given a notification fired 2 hours ago is in notification_log with user_action=null
  When the user opens/refreshes the PWA
  Then that alert appears in the Alerts panel without requiring a fresh evaluate() call

- Given the user dismisses an alert
  When dismiss(i) is called
  Then PATCH /notifications?id=<logId> is fired (non-blocking)
  And the alert disappears from UI immediately regardless of API response

- Given PATCH /notifications returns a network error
  When the user dismisses an alert
  Then the alert still disappears from UI (local state wins)
  And the error is console.warn only (non-fatal)

- Given notification_log has 3 in_app rows in last 24h:
    2 with user_action=null, 1 with user_action='dismissed'
  When the app opens and loadPersistedNotifications() runs
  Then only the 2 undismissed alerts appear in the panel

- Given evaluate() fires and loadPersistedNotifications() also loaded the same alert
  When both run in the same session
  Then the alert appears only once in the panel (de-duplication by notification_log id)

title: Implement PATCH /notifications and PATCH /recommendations Netlify endpoints
description:
Two new Netlify functions to close feedback loops that currently have no write path.

netlify/functions/notifications.js
  PATCH /.netlify/functions/notifications?id=<logId>
  Body: { action: "dismissed" | "accepted" | "ignored" }
  Writes user_action and action_at to notification_log row matching id + user_id.
  Response: { ok: true } or { error: string } with 400/404/500.
  Note: user_action and action_at columns already exist in notification_log (original schema).
  Note: a GET variant for notification persistence (load undismissed alerts on app open)
  can be added here too: GET /notifications?since=<ISO>&channel=in_app&action=null

netlify/functions/recommendations.js
  PATCH /.netlify/functions/recommendations
  Body: { historyId: number, ref: string, action: "accepted" | "dismissed" | "deferred" }
  Appends { ref, action, at: ISO } to recommendation_history.item_feedback jsonb array.
  Uses Supabase jsonb_insert or RPC to append without overwriting existing array.
  Response: { ok: true } or { error: string }.

Both endpoints use SUPABASE_USER_ID env var for user scoping (not auth headers —
single-user system).

## Scenarios

- Given notification_log row id=42 exists with user_action=null
  When PATCH /notifications?id=42 is called with body {action:"dismissed"}
  Then the row is updated: user_action='dismissed', action_at=now()
  And response is { ok: true }

- Given id=9999 does not exist in notification_log
  When PATCH /notifications?id=9999 is called
  Then response is { error: "not found" } with HTTP 404

- Given recommendation_history row id=10 exists with item_feedback=[]
  When PATCH /recommendations is called with {historyId:10, ref:"task:34", action:"dismissed"}
  Then item_feedback becomes [{ref:"task:34", action:"dismissed", at:<ISO>}]
  And a second call appends rather than overwrites

- Given PATCH /recommendations is called with missing historyId
  When the endpoint processes the request
  Then response is { error: "historyId required" } with HTTP 400

- Given PATCH /notifications is called with an invalid action value
  When the endpoint validates the request
  Then response is { error: "invalid action" } with HTTP 400
close_reason: notifications.js (PATCH + GET) and recommendations.js (PATCH append) implemented and pushed in 8d691b4/6f99417. 17/17 scenario checks passed: action validation, 404 on miss, append-not-overwrite for item_feedback, 400 on missing historyId.

title: evaluate-rules.js: replace hardcoded EOD with freeBlocks.js, add deadline priority gate, return notification_log.id
description:
Three targeted changes to evaluate-rules.js:

1. evalOpenBlock: replace hardcoded EOD
   Currently line 141: const eod = new Date().setHours(20, 0, 0, 0)
   Replace with a call to computeFreeBlocks() from netlify/lib/freeBlocks.js.
   evalOpenBlock must now read weekly_schedule, schedule_exceptions, planning_window,
   and active block_rejections from Supabase alongside its existing reads.
   The function gains a richer picture of when the user actually works.

2. evalDeadlineProximity: add priority gate for long-horizon warnings
   Currently: no priority filter — all tasks trigger all warn_hours.
   Add: if warn_hours > 48 AND condition_config.priority_max is set,
   skip tasks with priority > priority_max.
   This prevents 7-day early warnings from firing on low-priority tasks.
   Update the seed rule condition_config to:
     { "warn_hours": [168, 48, 24], "statuses_to_check": ["pending","in_progress"],
       "priority_max": 1 }
   (The seed rule update is a data change; apply via Supabase SQL editor.)
   Fallback: if priority_max is absent from condition_config, skip the gate (backward compat).

3. Return notification_log.id in fired array
   Currently evaluate-rules.js INSERTs into notification_log but does not return the id.
   The fired array items need the log row id so useRulesEngine.js can pass it to
   PATCH /notifications for dismiss write-back.
   Update the INSERT to use RETURNING id (Supabase insert with select: ['id']).
   Add id to each fired item in the response: { id, rule_id, channel, title, body, payload }.

Dependencies: freeBlocks.js utility (life-ow8), Migration A for new user_preferences columns,
Migration C for block_rejections table.

## Scenarios

- Given a user with working hours 09:00-18:00 and soft_ceiling 18:00, no events after 16:00
  When evalOpenBlock evaluates
  Then it finds the 16:00-18:00 block via freeBlocks.js (not the old 20:00 EOD)
  And the notification reason references that actual block window

- Given a task with deadline 5 days out, priority=2 (medium), and condition_config priority_max=1
  When evalDeadlineProximity evaluates with warn_hours=[168,48,24]
  Then the 168h warning does NOT fire (priority 2 > max 1)
  And the 48h and 24h warnings will fire when applicable (no priority gate on short windows)

- Given a task with deadline 5 days out, priority=1 (high), priority_max=1
  When evalDeadlineProximity evaluates
  Then the 168h warning DOES fire

- Given condition_config has no priority_max key (legacy config)
  When evalDeadlineProximity evaluates
  Then it behaves as before — all priorities fire for all warn_hours (backward compat)

- Given evaluate-rules.js fires a notification and inserts it into notification_log
  When the fired array is returned in the response
  Then each item includes an id field matching the inserted notification_log row id
  And useRulesEngine.js can read that id to pass to PATCH /notifications on dismiss
close_reason: 3 targeted changes implemented and pushed (d2bdc24): freeBlocks.js replaces hardcoded 20:00 EOD in evalOpenBlock; priority gate added to evalDeadlineProximity; notification_log INSERT returns id in fired array. 9/9 scenario checks passed.

title: Extract shared calendar sync logic + implement calendar-sync-scheduled.js nightly cron
description:
Google and iCal sync logic currently lives entirely in the on-demand functions. To support
a nightly scheduled sync, extract the core work into a shared library and create a
scheduled variant that runs both syncs automatically.

Files to create/modify:
  netlify/lib/calendarSyncLogic.js (new)
    - syncGoogle(supabase, userId, googleClientId, googleClientSecret)
      → { synced: bool, datesWritten: number, error?: string }
    - syncICal(supabase, userId, feedUrl)
      → { synced: bool, datesWritten: number, error?: string }
    Both functions contain the logic currently in calendar-sync.js and ical-sync.js.

  netlify/functions/calendar-sync.js (modify)
    Import syncGoogle from calendarSyncLogic.js. Remove duplicated logic.
    Behaviour unchanged externally.

  netlify/functions/ical-sync.js (modify)
    Import syncICal from calendarSyncLogic.js. Remove duplicated logic.
    Behaviour unchanged externally.

  netlify/functions/calendar-sync-scheduled.js (new)
    Scheduled Netlify function. Calls syncGoogle then syncICal. Logs results.
    Returns 200 with { googleResult, icalResult }.

  netlify.toml (modify)
    Add [[schedule]] block pointing to calendar-sync-scheduled.js.
    Cron: "0 6 * * *" (6am UTC daily).

Note: netlify.toml currently has zero schedule blocks — clean slate.

## Scenarios

- Given GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, and SUPABASE_* env vars are set
  When calendar-sync-scheduled.js runs
  Then both Google and iCal syncs execute
  And results are logged with datesWritten counts

- Given Google token refresh fails (invalid/expired refresh token)
  When the scheduled sync runs
  Then googleResult contains { synced: false, error: "token_refresh_failed" }
  And iCal sync still runs after the Google failure (non-fatal)

- Given apple_ical_url is null/empty in user_preferences
  When the scheduled sync runs
  Then icalResult contains { synced: false, error: "no_url" } with no exception thrown

- Given the [[schedule]] block is added to netlify.toml with cron "0 6 * * *"
  When `netlify build` is run locally
  Then the build succeeds with no errors related to the schedule configuration

- Given on-demand calendar-sync.js is called via GET after the refactor
  When the handler runs
  Then behaviour is identical to pre-refactor (response shape unchanged, events returned)
close_reason: Shared lib extracted, scheduled function deployed. Verified live: syncGoogle writes 5 dates, syncICal writes 6 dates, on-demand functions response unchanged.

title: Extend calendar-sync.js and ical-sync.js from 3-day to 14-day event window
description:
recommend.js queries calendar_snapshot for up to 7 days ahead. The on-demand sync
functions currently fetch only ~3 days. Extending to 14 days ensures the snapshot
covers the full recommendation window even when the nightly sync (Phase 1 calendar
sync issue) is missed for a few days.

Changes:
  calendar-sync.js: change threeDaysOut calculation to 14 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000.
    Also update the byDate filter — currently it only writes today and tomorrow to
    calendar_snapshot (lines ~141-142). Update to write ALL dates in the 14-day window.
  ical-sync.js: change windowEnd from +3 days to +14 days.
    Update windowEndStr accordingly.

Both functions already use merge-on-upsert for calendar_snapshot rows (preserving
other-source events). That pattern is unchanged.

Note: Google Calendar API has maxResults=50. If a user has >50 events in 14 days,
events beyond 50 will be silently dropped. Add a console.warn if items.length >= 50.

## Scenarios

- Given a Google Calendar with an event on day 10 from today
  When calendar-sync.js runs after this change
  Then the event is written to calendar_snapshot
  And recommend.js can see it in its 7-day query

- Given an iCal feed with an event on day 12 from today
  When ical-sync.js runs
  Then the event appears in calendar_snapshot with the correct date

- Given the 14-day Google window returns exactly 50 events (the API cap)
  When calendar-sync.js processes the response
  Then a console.warn is logged indicating the cap was hit

- Given calendar_snapshot already has Apple events for a date in the 14-day window
  When calendar-sync.js upserts Google events for that same date
  Then the Apple events are preserved (merge-on-upsert behaviour unchanged)
close_reason: 14-day window live. Verified: calendar-sync returns events May 25→Jun 4 (5 dates); ical-sync May 24→Jun 4 (6 dates); calendar_snapshot has 9 rows across both sources.

title: Deduplicate calendar events by UID across sources before sending to Claude
description:
If the user's iCal feed overlaps with their Google Calendar (e.g. a shared calendar visible in both), the same event appears twice in calendar_snapshot — once with source='google' and once with source='apple'. Claude sees duplicate events and can miscount busy time or double-weight a meeting.

What needs to change:
- In recommend.js, when building calendarLines from calendar_snapshot rows, deduplicate events by their id field before rendering the prompt. Google event IDs and iCal UIDs are distinct strings — keep the first occurrence, drop subsequent ones with the same id.

## Scenarios

- Given calendar_snapshot for May 23 contains two events with the same id (one source=google, one source=apple)
  When recommend.js builds the calendar prompt section
  Then only one instance of the event appears in the prompt

- Given all events have distinct ids
  When recommend.js builds the calendar section
  Then all events are included with no change
close_reason: All three deployed to https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app. node-ical handles TZID correctly; recommend.js reads user_preferences.timezone for prompt, calendar query, and event formatting; events deduplicated by id before Claude sees them.

title: Fix today/tomorrow bucketing to use browser local date
description:
The server assigns 'today'/'tomorrow' based on UTC date. Users in non-UTC timezones see events in the wrong bucket or missing entirely — e.g. at 9 PM ET (1 AM UTC next day) the server's todayStr is already tomorrow, so tonight's events get dropped.

Root cause: both calendar-sync and ical-sync filter and label events using now.toISOString().split('T')[0] (UTC). The PWA trusts that date field for display.

Fix:
- Server: widen the response to send all events in the 3-day fetch window (remove the strict UTC today/tomorrow filter from the API response; keep per-date snapshot writes as-is)
- ical-sync: same — widen normalizeEvents to pass through events in a 3-day window rather than just UTC today/tomorrow
- PWA: after receiving events, recompute the date field from startISO using browser local time; filter to local today/tomorrow only

## Scenarios

- Given the server clock is UTC 2026-05-24T01:00:00Z (1 AM UTC)
  When the user's browser is in America/New_York (still May 23 locally, 9 PM ET)
  Then events on May 23 appear under Today and events on May 24 appear under Tomorrow

- Given events exist for the next 3 calendar days
  When the PWA loads
  Then only local-today and local-tomorrow events appear in the calendar panel; day-3 events are excluded

- Given an all-day event with no startISO
  When the PWA rebuckets events
  Then it falls back to the server-assigned date field without crashing
close_reason: rebucketByLocalDate() applied in both sync hooks; server sends full 3-day window. Deployed.

title: Fix calendar event times to display in browser local timezone
description:
Calendar events show times extracted server-side as HH:MM strings. The Netlify function runs in UTC, so events are not converted to the user's local timezone before display.

Root cause: both calendar-sync.js and ical-sync.js strip timestamps down to HH:MM on the server and send those strings to the PWA. The PWA displays them verbatim. The browser never gets an ISO timestamp it can convert.

Fix: pass full ISO timestamps (startISO, endISO) from both sync functions to the PWA alongside the existing HH:MM fields. In CalendarContent, format display time using new Date(startISO).toLocaleTimeString() so the browser applies the user's local timezone.

Scope:
- calendar-sync.js: add startISO (item.start.dateTime) to returned event shape
- ical-sync.js: fix parseDateVal to return correct ISO strings — UTC events keep Z suffix, TZID/floating events omit Z so the browser treats them as local time (best-effort without a tz database; node-ical swap in a follow-up will improve this)
- LifeOrganizer.jsx CalendarContent: format time from startISO when present, fall back to start string

## Scenarios

- Given a Google Calendar event stored as 2026-05-23T14:30:00-04:00
  When the PWA loads in a browser set to America/New_York (UTC-4)
  Then CalendarContent displays the event start time as 14:30 (offset already encodes local time)

- Given a Google Calendar event stored as 2026-05-23T18:30:00Z (UTC)
  When the PWA loads in a browser set to America/New_York (UTC-4)
  Then CalendarContent displays 14:30, not 18:30

- Given an iCal event with DTSTART:20260523T183000Z
  When the PWA loads in a browser set to America/New_York (UTC-4)
  Then CalendarContent displays 14:30

- Given an iCal event with DTSTART;TZID=America/New_York:20260523T143000
  When the PWA loads in a browser set to America/New_York
  Then CalendarContent displays 14:30 (floating local time treated as browser local — correct for matching timezone)
close_reason: startISO/endISO added to both sync functions; PWA formats with toLocaleTimeString(). Deployed to https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app

title: Add Apple iCal feed support
description:
The calendar panel currently only shows Google Calendar events. Users with Apple Calendar (iCloud) or any CalDAV-backed calendar have no integration path.

Add support for subscribing to an iCal (.ics) feed URL. The user pastes a calendar share URL (from Apple Calendar, Fantastical, Outlook, etc.) into Settings, and events are fetched + normalized into the same calendar_snapshot table alongside Google events.

What needs to be done:
- DB migration: add apple_ical_url column to user_preferences (run manually in Supabase dashboard — no CLI setup in this project)
- Netlify function ical-sync.js: fetch + parse the .ics feed, upsert to calendar_snapshot with source='apple', merge-on-write so Google events are not overwritten
- Netlify function ical-save-url.js: POST endpoint to save/clear the URL in user_preferences
- Update calendar-sync.js to merge-on-upsert (keep non-Google events) so sources coexist
- PWA: useICalSync hook + Settings UI input (with webcal:// → https:// normalisation) + merge events into CalendarContent

## Scenarios

- Given apple_ical_url is not set in user_preferences
  When GET /.netlify/functions/ical-sync is called
  Then it returns { connected: false, events: [] } without making any external request

- Given apple_ical_url is set and the .ics URL is reachable
  When GET /.netlify/functions/ical-sync is called
  Then it parses events for today and tomorrow, writes them with source='apple' to calendar_snapshot (preserving any existing google events for that date), and returns { connected: true, events: [...] }

- Given both Google and iCal are configured
  When the PWA loads and both calendar-sync and ical-sync complete
  Then CalendarContent shows events from both sources merged and sorted by start time

- Given the user pastes a webcal:// URL into the iCal URL input and saves
  When ical-save-url normalises and stores the URL
  Then ical-sync fetches it over https:// and returns events correctly

- Given the user clears the iCal URL input and saves an empty string
  When ical-sync is called next
  Then it returns { connected: false, events: [] } and no Apple events appear in the calendar panel
close_reason: All code shipped and pushed. Migration file at supabase/migrations/20260523000000_add_apple_ical_url.sql must be applied in the Supabase dashboard before ical-sync will write to DB.

title: Inbox tabs: Manual / Beads / All
description:
## Why
The unified inbox is too long with many Beads projects. Tabs separate
Manual (personal tasks) and Beads (dev queue) into distinct views while
keeping All for the smart unified sort.

## Scenarios
- Given the app loads, the Manual tab is active showing only manual tasks
- Given the user taps Beads, only Beads issues are shown
- Given the user taps All, the full unified sorted list appears
- Given a QuickStats filter is active, it applies within the current tab
close_reason: Tabs deployed. Manual/Beads/All tab bar with per-source counts. Status filter applies within each tab as a dismissible chip. Priority cutoff removed — no longer needed.

title: Persist tasks to Supabase open_tasks
description:
## Why

Tasks are currently stored only in localStorage — per-device, per-browser, lost on cache clear.
The Supabase open_tasks table already exists (from the world state schema) but the UI never
reads or writes to it. This makes task data fragile and prevents future features like email
intake populating tasks.

## What

Wire addTask, updateStatus, deleteTask, and completeTask to open_tasks in Supabase via a new
tasks.js Netlify function. Use optimistic updates so the UI stays snappy. Migrate any existing
localStorage tasks on first load.

## Scenarios

- Given a user adds a task via the form
  When the task is submitted
  Then it appears immediately in the list (optimistic) and is persisted to open_tasks in Supabase

- Given tasks exist in Supabase
  When the app is loaded on a fresh browser (no localStorage)
  Then tasks are loaded from Supabase and displayed correctly

- Given localStorage has tasks from before this change
  When the app loads for the first time after the update
  Then localStorage tasks are migrated to Supabase and localStorage is cleared

- Given the user changes a task status or deletes a task
  When the action is taken
  Then the UI updates immediately (optimistic) and the change is written to Supabase in the background
close_reason: tasks.js CRUD function deployed, LifeOrganizer.jsx switched from localStorage to Supabase with optimistic updates + localStorage migration. Verified build passes. Category migration SQL written — needs one manual run in Supabase SQL editor.

title: Decide + implement per-rule notification channel config
description:
## Why
The unified inbox is too long with many Beads projects. Tabs separate
Manual (personal tasks) and Beads (dev queue) into distinct views while
keeping All for the smart unified sort.

life-xnb (Email dispatch) requires email to be opted in per rule. life-qlr (Web Push) and life-4ov (in-app) need the same. The rules table currently has no column to store which channels a rule fires to.

Decision: Option A — add notification_channels jsonb column to rules table. The distinction between 'when to fire' (condition_config) and 'where to route' (notification_channels) is meaningful and worth a separate column.

## Scenarios

- Given the migration runs, when rules exist with no prior notification_channels value, then all rows have notification_channels = '["in_app"]' (the column default).
- Given Rule 3 (issue_unblocked) has notify_channel inside condition_config, when the migration runs, then notify_channel is stripped from condition_config and notification_channels is set to '["in_app"]'.
- Given evaluate-rules evaluates a rule whose notification_channels is ["in_app","email"], when a condition matches, then two notification_log entries are inserted — one with channel='in_app' and one with channel='email'.
- Given evaluate-rules evaluates a rule and notification_channels is null/missing, when a condition matches, then channel defaults to 'in_app'.
notes: SEED DATA INCONSISTENCY: Rule 3 (issue_unblocked) already uses notify_channel inside condition_config: {priority_max:1, notify_channel:'in_app'}. This is exactly Option B data. The migration must also clean this up — strip notify_channel from condition_config and write it to notification_channels instead (if Option A is chosen). EVALUATE-RULES.JS MUST BE UPDATED: all four condition evaluators currently hardcode channel:'in_app' in their return objects. When this issue is implemented, evaluate-rules.js must be updated to read rule.notification_channels and set channel accordingly. This is not mentioned in the description. life-4ov DEPENDENCY MISSING: life-4ov (in-app notifications) also needs to know which rules route to in_app channel — add bd dep add life-4ov life-v66 after this is resolved.
close_reason: notification_channels jsonb column added to rules (default ["in_app"]). Migration run on production Supabase — all 4 rules have column set, issue_unblocked rule cleaned of stray notify_channel in condition_config. evaluate-rules.js updated to read rule.notification_channels and expand one log entry per channel. life-4ov dependency wired.

title: Migration: add source column to calendar_snapshot
description:
life-36d (Corporate Bridge) writes corporate events to calendar_snapshot with source='corporate'. The current calendar_snapshot schema has no source column — only id, user_id, event_date, events (jsonb), created_at, updated_at.

Without this column, there is no way to distinguish personal Google Calendar events from corporate iCal events in the snapshot.

**Chosen approach: Option B (per-event source tag in JSONB)**
Keep one row per (user_id, event_date). Add source field inside each event object in the events JSONB array: {id, title, start, end, source, ...}. calendar-sync.js tags its events with source='google'. The corporate bridge (life-36d) will tag its events with source='corporate'. evalOpenBlock in evaluate-rules.js is unchanged — it already treats events as a flat array.

No ALTER TABLE needed. No UNIQUE constraint change needed.

## Scenarios

- Given Google Calendar has events on today's date
  When calendar-sync runs and builds the byDate map
  Then each event object pushed to calendar_snapshot.events JSONB includes source='google'

- Given a day's calendar_snapshot already contains source='google' events
  When life-36d's corporate bridge writes iCal events for the same date
  Then corporate events are tagged source='corporate' and the two sets are distinguishable without any schema change

- Given calendar_snapshot rows contain events with source='google' and source='corporate'
  When evalOpenBlock detects free gaps from today's events
  Then all events regardless of source are included in gap detection (no regression in Rules Engine)

- Given older calendar_snapshot rows have event objects without a source field
  When evaluate-rules.js reads them
  Then the missing source field causes no errors (JS reads undefined, not a throw)
notes: STRUCTURAL FLAW IN MIGRATION AS WRITTEN: calendar_snapshot has UNIQUE (user_id, event_date) — only one row per day. Adding source as a row-level column means a second INSERT for the same date (e.g. corporate iCal events on a day that already has Google events) violates the constraint. Two valid approaches: OPTION A (row-per-source): Change UNIQUE (user_id, event_date) to UNIQUE (user_id, event_date, source). Then update evalOpenBlock in evaluate-rules.js to merge all matching rows for today (currently uses .find() which returns only the first). OPTION B (per-event source tag): Keep one row per date. Add source field inside each event object in the events JSONB array: {id, title, start, end, source, ...}. Context Collector merges Google and corporate events into one row, each event tagged. Rules Engine unchanged. RECOMMENDATION: Option B — the Rules Engine already handles events as a flat array, no code changes needed there. Implement as: no row-level column migration needed; add source to event objects when writing.
close_reason: Option B implemented: calendar-sync.js now tags every Google Calendar event with source='google' in the JSONB. life-36d can write source='corporate' events into the same row. No schema change, no UNIQUE constraint change, evalOpenBlock unchanged. Pushed in 3a7267c.

title: Add POST /api/beads/create endpoint to Beads Service
description:
life-lqn (Conversation Intake Agent) routes new Beads issues through the Beads Service REST API, but server/index.js has no create endpoint. Current routes are: health, sync, ready, list, show/:id, claim/:id, close/:id, stats. This must be added before life-lqn can be implemented.

Implementation:
1. Add POST /api/beads/create to server/index.js
2. Accept body: { title, description, type, priority, labels } (matches bd create flags; labels is optional)
3. Execute bd create via execSync — NOT via the bd() helper (which parses arrays; bd create --json returns a single object {})
4. Escape title, description, and labels with the same single-quote escaping already used by /api/beads/close/:id: value.replace(/'/g, "'\\''" )
5. Return the created issue JSON object
6. Auth: existing Bearer token middleware already covers it

## Scenarios

- Given a POST /api/beads/create request with { title: 'Fix login bug', type: 'bug', priority: 1 }
  When the handler runs
  Then bd create is called with the correct flags, the response is the created issue JSON object (id, title, status, etc.), and HTTP 201 is returned

- Given a title containing a single quote (e.g. Mo's task)
  When the handler escapes and passes it to bd create
  Then the command does not break and the issue is created with the literal title Mo's task (no shell injection)

- Given a POST with no title field
  When the handler validates the body
  Then HTTP 400 is returned with { error: 'title is required' } and no bd create is called

- Given a valid request with an optional labels field (e.g. labels: ['life-organizer'])
  When the handler constructs the bd create command
  Then --labels life-organizer is appended and the created issue carries that label
design:
## Scenarios

  When the handler runs
  Then bd create is called with the correct flags, the response is the created issue JSON object (id, title, status, etc.), and HTTP 201 is returned

- Given a title containing a single quote (e.g. "Mo's task")
  When the handler escapes and passes it to bd create
  Then the command does not break and the issue is created with the literal title Mo's task (no shell injection)

- Given a POST with no title field
  When the handler validates the body
  Then HTTP 400 is returned with { error: 'title is required' } and no bd create is called

- Given a valid request with an optional labels field (e.g. labels: ["life-organizer"])

notes:
IMPLEMENTATION DECISIONS (resolved during drift analysis 2026-05-25):

Escaping strategy: use single-quote escaping — value.replace(/'/g, "'\\''" ) — consistent with the existing /api/beads/close/:id pattern. No temp files, no stdin-json needed.

bd create --json output shape: returns a single JSON OBJECT {}, not an array []. Do NOT use the bd() helper (which does lastIndexOf(']') array-parsing). Use execSync directly and JSON.parse the raw output.

labels field: bd create --labels is supported. Add labels as an optional body field so callers (e.g. life-lqn) can tag issues with the correct project label.

SHELL INJECTION RISK (still applies): title, description, and labels are free-text user input interpolated into a shell string. All three must be escaped before interpolation.
close_reason: Deployed to Railway production. All 4 scenarios pass: happy path returns 201+JSON, single-quote injection handled correctly (Mo's task preserved), missing title returns 400, labels field wired. Uses execSync+JSON.parse (not bd() helper) per drift analysis finding that bd create --json returns {} not [].

title: bdg read-path gap: bd repo sync changes not pushed to DoltHub
description:
When bdg runs a read command (list, ready, show, stats, etc.), bd repo sync pulls project changes into beads-global's local Dolt DB. But because reads don't trigger the post-commit hook, those synced changes sit in beads-global's local Dolt and never reach DoltHub — so Railway and Supabase stay stale until a write happens to come along.

Reproduction:
1. Close an issue in a project repo via `cd project && bd close <id>`
2. Run `bdg ready` — bd repo sync pulls the close into beads-global
3. Check DoltHub / Supabase — issue still shows as open (no push fired)
4. Run any bdg write — NOW the push fires and Supabase updates

Root cause:
The post-commit hook is only triggered in the write path of bdg(). The read path calls bd repo sync but has no mechanism to detect whether that sync produced new Dolt commits.

Fix:
After bd repo sync in the read path, check whether beads-global has unpushed commits. If it does, fire the post-commit hook. Candidate check:

  before=$(cd ~/beads-global && bd dolt status --json 2>/dev/null)
  bd repo sync >/dev/null
  after=$(cd ~/beads-global && bd dolt status --json 2>/dev/null)
  if [[ "$before" != "$after" ]]; then
    ~/beads-global/.beads/hooks/post-commit &
  fi

Or simpler: always fire the hook after bd repo sync, relying on the debounce to prevent hammering. The debounce trailing-fallback already handles burst suppression safely.

Depends on: none — standalone improvement to bdg() in ~/.zshrc
notes: WRITE-PATH GAP (related to read-path gap): the project-level post-commit hook (.beads/hooks/post-commit) fires immediately after a local bd write and calls the Context Collector. But Railway reads from DoltHub, and the chain from project write to DoltHub is: project .beads/ → bd repo sync in ~/beads-global → bd dolt push → DoltHub → Railway bd dolt pull. The hook fires before bd repo sync and bd dolt push have run, so Railway syncs from a stale DoltHub. Result: PWA shows closed issues as still open until a manual bd repo sync + bd dolt push + Railway sync is run. Correct fix: the hook should first run bd repo sync + bd dolt push, THEN call the Context Collector. Or the hook should live at the beads-global level, firing AFTER bdg pushes to DoltHub. Workaround: after any project-level bd write, run: cd ~/beads-global && bd repo sync && bd dolt push — then the hook (or a manual trigger) will pick up correct state.
close_reason: Fixed in ~/.zshrc default path. Changed '[ "$cmd" = "repo" ] || bd repo sync' to an if/fi block that fires post-commit hook after sync. Verified in fresh zsh session: bdg ready now updates ~/.beads-post-commit-last (hook fired: YES). Debounce protects against burst reads.

title: Dolt post-commit hook: trigger Context Collector on bd write
description:
Wire a Dolt post-commit hook so that any local `bd` write (close, claim, create) automatically triggers the Context Collector Netlify function, keeping World State in near-real-time sync with Beads data.

Why deferred from life-4qr:
The Context Collector function (life-4qr) will expose an HTTP endpoint callable by anything. The hook itself is 3 lines of shell. Deferred because: (a) the function must exist first, (b) the hook fires on every bd write with no debounce — a busy session would hammer the collector — and (c) it introduces Dolt hooks infrastructure not yet tested.

Implementation:
1. Add debounce logic: track last-fired timestamp, skip if < N seconds since last call (suggest 30s).
2. Write .beads/hooks/post-commit:
   #!/bin/bash
   # Trigger Context Collector when Beads data changes.
   # Debounce: skip if fired within last 30s.
   LAST=$(cat ~/.beads-hook-last 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
   NOW=$(date +%s)
   if [ $((NOW - LAST)) -lt 30 ]; then exit 0; fi
   echo $NOW > ~/.beads-hook-last
   curl -s -X POST https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/collect-world-state \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer $BEADS_API_KEY" > /dev/null &
3. Make hook executable: chmod +x .beads/hooks/post-commit
4. Test: run bd close on a test issue, confirm World State updated in Supabase within ~5s.
5. Document BEADS_API_KEY requirement in CLAUDE.md (must be set in shell env for hook to auth).

Depends on: life-4qr (Netlify function must exist and be deployed first)

## Scenarios

- Given BEADS_API_KEY is set in the shell environment and life-4qr's Netlify function is deployed
  When `bd close` is run on any issue
  Then the post-commit hook fires, the Context Collector runs, and beads_ready in Supabase is updated within 5 seconds

- Given the hook fired less than 30 seconds ago
  When another bd write occurs
  Then the hook skips the HTTP call (debounce) and exits silently

- Given the Netlify function is unreachable when the hook fires
  When the curl call fails
  Then the hook exits silently — the bd operation completes normally and is not blocked
notes: INCORRECT IMPLEMENTATION: hook fires immediately after a project bd write and calls the Context Collector. But Railway reads from DoltHub, and the project changes haven't reached DoltHub yet at that moment. Chain required before calling Netlify: (1) cd ~/beads-global && bd repo sync, (2) bd dolt push, (3) THEN curl to Context Collector. Without steps 1-2, the Railway sync always pulls stale data. LATENCY CONCERN: adding repo sync + dolt push to every bd write will slow the hook by several seconds. This is acceptable — the alternative is a hook that silently does nothing useful. CORRECT HOOK BODY: replace the current curl block with: cd ~/beads-global && bd repo sync > /dev/null 2>&1 && bd dolt push > /dev/null 2>&1 && curl -s -X POST COLLECTOR_URL ... VERIFICATION: close a real issue, watch Supabase beads_ready update within ~15s. That is the actual acceptance test.
close_reason: Fixed: hook now runs bd repo sync + bd dolt push in ~/beads-global before calling Context Collector. End-to-end verified.

title: Email dispatch: Rules Engine → Resend for high-priority alerts
description:
Email delivery channel in the Notification Dispatcher. When the Rules Engine fires a high-priority rule match, send an email alert via Resend.

Architecture:
Replaces the original life-7pf (standalone Netlify cron with hardcoded deadline check). Email dispatch is now triggered by the Rules Engine, not a separate scheduled function. Any rule can be configured to notify via email channel.

Implementation:
1. Create Resend account (resend.com — free tier 3000 emails/month).
2. Add RESEND_API_KEY to Netlify env vars.
3. Create Netlify function: POST /api/notify/email with {rule_match, user_email, context}.
4. Template: subject from rule match type, body includes task title + deadline + context + direct link to PWA.
5. Deduplication: check notification_log before sending (same rule + same task + same day = skip).
6. Respect user_preferences.quiet_hours (don't send between quiet_hours_start and quiet_hours_end).
7. Channel selection: email is opted-in per rule (configurable in Settings).

title: Web Push infrastructure: background notifications via service worker
description:
True background push notifications — fires even when the PWA is not open. Requires service worker + VAPID keys + push subscription stored server-side.

Architecture:
The Rules Engine fires a rule match → Notification Dispatcher routes to push channel → Netlify function sends Web Push message → service worker receives and displays notification.

This is the background complement to the in-app notifications in life-4ov.

Implementation:
1. Generate VAPID key pair (web-push library).
2. Add VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY and VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY to Netlify env vars.
3. Service worker: handle 'push' event → display notification with title + body + action buttons.
4. PWA: on permission grant, call pushManager.subscribe() with VAPID public key. Store subscription endpoint in Supabase user_preferences.
5. Netlify function: given a rule match, call web-push.sendNotification(subscription, payload).
6. Integrate with Notification Dispatcher: push channel is selected based on user preference and notification priority.

Note: requires HTTPS in production (already satisfied by Netlify). iOS Safari support requires iOS 16.4+ with PWA installed to home screen.

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1), life-57m (World State schema — needs subscription storage)
Part of: EPIC-NOTIFY (life-k6l)

Add as VITE_VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY (Netlify env var) and reference as import.meta.env.VITE_VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY in the PWA. VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY stays server-side only (no VITE_ prefix). title: Conversation Intake Agent: Claude session → Beads issues and tasks
description:
Build the Conversation Intake Agent: extracts tasks, commitments, and decisions from conversational text and creates Beads issues or personal tasks.

Why:
This closes the loop between thinking and doing. Conversations (with Claude, solo journaling, notes) are where commitments are made — but they currently disappear. This agent makes capture explicit, structured, and automatic.

Trigger: Explicit user invocation only (v1). User pastes conversation text into PWA form. Session-end automatic capture deferred until confidence gating is calibrated.

Input schema:
{ text: string, source: 'claude-session'|'note'|'voice-transcript'|'email'|'form', context?: 'work'|'personal'|'mixed', project?: string, sourceUrl?: string }

project?: string — the project label to apply when destination='beads'. Selected by the user at submit time via a project dropdown in ConversationIntakeWidget. Passed as labels: [project] to POST /api/beads/create. When omitted, no label is applied (issue lands in beads-global unlabelled).

Claude API call:
Single structured extraction call. Conservative system prompt defining TASK / COMMITMENT / DECISION / QUESTION / EVENT types. Output: typed JSON array of Extraction objects each containing { type, content: {title, description, deadline, priority}, destination: 'beads'|'personal-task', project?, thirdParty?: bool, confidence: 0.0-1.0, sourceSpan }

thirdParty: true when Claude identifies a named non-user actor as the doer (e.g. 'Sarah will handle X'). Forces the item into NEEDS REVIEW regardless of confidence score.

Confidence gate:
- >= 0.80: auto-create without user confirmation (except thirdParty items, which always go to review)
- 0.50-0.79: surface in 'needs review' UI panel for user approval
- < 0.50: skip (shown collapsed under 'Low confidence (N)' for transparency)

Output routing:
- destination='beads': POST to /api/beads/create on Railway Beads Service with labels=[project] if project set
- destination='personal-task': POST to /.netlify/functions/tasks → INSERT to Supabase open_tasks
- Source context determines destination hint: work conversation → beads; personal → tasks; mixed → Claude decides per item

Long conversations: truncate at ~30 000 tokens (conservative for a single extraction call on claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 with 200K context). Note truncation in the response so the UI can show a 'conversation was trimmed' warning.

Edge cases to handle:
- All questions → extract as QUESTION type, never auto-created, shown under 'Questions raised'
- Past deadlines → downgrade to review regardless of confidence
- 'Sarah will handle X' → extract with thirdParty: true, surface for confirmation regardless of confidence
- Long conversations → truncate at ~30 000 tokens, note truncation in response

Walk-through example
────────────────────
User pastes this Claude session excerpt:
  'Let's get the rate-limiting fix merged before the API deploy on Friday — that's P1.
   Also I should probably book a dentist appointment. And Sarah said she'll handle
   the vendor invoice by Wednesday.'

Submitted with: source='claude-session', context='mixed', project='life-organizer'

Claude extracts:
  [
    { type: 'TASK', title: 'Fix rate-limiting before API deploy',
      content: { priority: 1, deadline: 'Friday' },
      destination: 'beads', confidence: 0.91,
      sourceSpan: 'get the rate-limiting fix merged before the API deploy on Friday' },

    { type: 'TASK', title: 'Book dentist appointment',
      content: { priority: 3 },
      destination: 'personal-task', confidence: 0.63,
      sourceSpan: 'I should probably book a dentist appointment' },

    { type: 'TASK', title: 'Sarah to send vendor invoice',
      content: { owner: 'Sarah', deadline: 'Wednesday' },
      destination: 'beads', confidence: 0.71, thirdParty: true,
      sourceSpan: 'Sarah said she will handle the vendor invoice by Wednesday' }
  ]

Actions taken:
  Item 1 (confidence 0.91 >= 0.80):  AUTO-CREATED via POST /api/beads/create
      { title: 'Fix rate-limiting before API deploy', priority: 1, labels: ['life-organizer'] }
  Item 2 (confidence 0.63, 0.50-0.79):  NEEDS REVIEW panel (personal-task)
  Item 3 (confidence 0.71, thirdParty=true):  NEEDS REVIEW panel (beads)
      UI shows: 'Owner: Sarah — confirm before creating'

UI components needed:
- ConversationIntakeWidget: textarea + source selector + context selector + project dropdown + submit button
- ConversationIntakeResult: auto-created list + needs-review checklist with approve/dismiss per item + questions-raised list + low-confidence collapsed section

Part of: EPIC-INTAKE (life-72y)

## Scenarios

- Given text: 'Fix the rate-limiting bug before Friday API deploy — P1'
         with context='work', project='life-organizer'
  When  the intake form is submitted
  Then  Claude returns confidence >= 0.80 for a TASK extraction
  And   POST /api/beads/create fires automatically with labels=['life-organizer']
  And   the result panel shows the created issue ID under 'Created automatically'

- Given text: 'I should probably book a dentist appointment'
         with context='personal'
  When  submitted
  Then  Claude returns 0.50 <= confidence < 0.80
  And   the item appears in the 'Needs review' checklist (NOT auto-created)
  And   user can approve to POST /.netlify/functions/tasks and create it in open_tasks
  And   user can dismiss and the item is discarded with no DB write

- Given text: 'We should maybe think about rewriting auth at some point'
  When  submitted
  Then  Claude returns confidence < 0.50
  And   the item is NOT created and NOT shown in needs-review
  And   it appears collapsed under 'Low confidence (1)' for transparency

- Given text: 'Should we migrate to TypeScript? Worth thinking about.'
  When  submitted
  Then  Claude extracts { type: 'QUESTION', ... }
  And   QUESTION type items are never auto-created regardless of confidence
  And   the result shows them under 'Questions raised' without a create button

- Given text: 'Sarah said she will send the contract by Wednesday'
  When  submitted with any context
  Then  Claude sets thirdParty: true on the extraction
  And   the item is routed to NEEDS REVIEW even if confidence >= 0.80
  And   the review panel shows 'Owner: Sarah — confirm before creating'

- Given the intake Netlify function receives a POST with no text field
  When  the handler validates the body
  Then  it returns HTTP 400 { error: 'text is required' } immediately
  And   no Claude API call is made
notes:
life-rvn (POST /api/beads/create) is now closed — blocker resolved.
Token limit resolved: ~30k tokens is the practical cap for a haiku extraction call (200K context window).
thirdParty flag: any extraction where Claude identifies a named non-user actor as the doer must set thirdParty: true, which forces the item into NEEDS REVIEW regardless of confidence score.
close_reason: Deployed to production: intake.js (Claude haiku extraction) + beads-create.js (Railway proxy) + ConversationIntakeWidget/Result components. E2E tested: walk-through example extracts correctly (confidence routing matches spec), beads-create proxy creates real issues, missing-text returns 400 with no Claude call.

title: Corporate Bridge: iCal subscription + email forwarding setup
description:
Read-only integration with corporate (Comcast) calendar and email via pragmatic bridges that require no IT involvement.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Corporate Walled Garden):
Full Exchange/Outlook integration requires IT approval and corporate OAuth. The ceiling is iCal URL subscription + user-configured forwarding rules — ~70% of scheduling value at zero IT involvement.

Component 1: iCal URL subscription
  Outlook allows publishing a read-only iCal URL: Settings → Calendar → Shared calendars → Publish.
  Subscribe this URL in the Calendar Context Collector alongside Google Calendar.
  Provides: events, free/busy, meeting titles. Sufficient for scheduling.
  No write operations. No OAuth.

Component 2: Email forwarding rules (user-configured, documented)
  User sets up Outlook rule: selected senders or subjects → forward to personal Gmail.
  Gmail Intake Agent (life-9br) picks up forwarded emails automatically.
  Life Organizer does not configure these — just documents the setup and handles forwarded emails gracefully.

Implementation:

title: Email Intake Agent: Gmail parsing → World State tasks
description:
Claude-powered agent that reads personal Gmail, classifies each email, extracts actionable items, and writes them to World State.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Intake Layer as a First-Class Architectural Concern):
Email is where a large fraction of real commitments live — often implicit, rarely formatted as tasks. Rules can't parse 'could you take a look before Friday?' — Claude identifies the implicit ask, estimates urgency from sender context, and creates a task with appropriate confidence score.

Classification categories:
  meeting invite → create calendar event + optional prep task
  explicit deadline → create task with extracted deadline
  implicit ask → create task flagged low-confidence, surface for user confirmation
  notification/FYI → log to World State context, no task
  noise → discard

Implementation:
1. Gmail OAuth: add gmail.readonly scope to existing Google OAuth flow.
2. Poll Gmail (on schedule or app-open) for unprocessed emails (last 24h).
3. For each email: Claude call with subject + sender + body → classification JSON.
4. High-confidence results: write to Supabase tasks directly.
5. Low-confidence results: surface in PWA as 'Did you mean to add this?' prompt.
6. Track processed emails (message ID) to avoid reprocessing.
7. User can adjust classification rules in Settings.

Trigger: scheduled (daily) + manual ('check email for tasks' button in PWA)

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1 — output goes to World State which rules evaluate)
Part of: EPIC-INTAKE (life-72y)

These are two different OAuth flows. Do not extend the client-side Calendar OAuth. Gmail API uses quota units (not raw request counts) and the free tier is much higher. Verify actual quota at console.cloud.google.com → APIs & Services → Gmail API. title: Pattern Analyzer: daily Claude call for rule refinement
description:
Build the Pattern Analyzer: a weekly Claude call that reviews rule firing history and suggests rule improvements.

Why:
Rules are the executable memory of learned behaviour. Without a feedback loop, rules never improve. The Pattern Analyzer closes that loop: one Claude call per week reviews what fired, how users responded, and suggests threshold/cooldown adjustments or new rules.

Trigger: Netlify scheduled function, weekly (Monday 2am UTC).
  netlify/functions/pattern-analyzer.mts with config: { schedule: '0 2 * * 1' }

Input (read from Supabase):
- rules table: active rules with total_fires, accepted_count, dismissed_count, deprecated_at
- notification_log: per-rule firing history with user_action, action_at, action_latency_seconds
- Computed: acceptance rate, dismissal rate, avg action latency, fires-by-hour pattern

Claude API call:
Single call per cycle. Conservative system prompt: only suggest changes with clear evidence (high-volume dismissal, temporal pattern, rule conflict). Returns 1-2 high-confidence suggestions max as JSON.

Suggestion types:
- threshold_adjustment: raise/lower priority_threshold
- cooldown_adjustment: change cooldown_minutes
- new_rule: formalise an observed pattern not yet captured
- deprecate: disable a rule with near-zero acceptance rate

Output stored in: Supabase rule_suggestions table
Fields: suggestion_type, target_rule_id, original_value jsonb, suggested_value jsonb, reasoning, confidence, approved (null=pending)

Human review vs auto-apply:
- Phase 4 launch: ALL suggestions require human approval (email link to approve/reject)
- Trust mode (later): confidence >= 0.85 auto-applies; rest surface for review
- Hard cap: max 2 auto-applied changes per cycle

Feedback loop prevention:
- Never suggest the same change twice within 30 days
- If rule changed in last 7 days, require >= 0.90 confidence before re-suggesting

Notification: email to user when suggestions are ready (via Resend, same as EPIC-NOTIFY)

Depends on: Rules Engine v1 firing history must exist (life-b45)
Part of: EPIC-INTEL (life-39b)

title: Notification & Dispatch
description:
The Notification Dispatcher: routes Rules Engine output to the right channel (in-app, push, email) with appropriate priority and cooldown management.

Components:
1. In-app notifications (life-4ov revised): browser Notification API, Rules Engine-triggered, cooldown via notification_log
2. Web Push infrastructure: service worker registration, VAPID keys, push server (Netlify function or dedicated), background delivery
3. Email dispatch via Resend: Rules Engine → email for high-priority rule matches. Resend free tier (3000/month). Replaces standalone cron approach.

Design principle: the dispatcher is dumb routing. Intelligence (what to notify) lives in the Rules Engine. The dispatcher only decides how and whether to route (channel, deduplication, quiet hours).

Depends on: EPIC-INTEL (Rules Engine must exist to trigger dispatch)

See ARD v2 §Decision 10 and §Rules Engine Pattern.

title: Intake Agents
description:
Agents that convert unstructured, ambiguous external signals into structured World State entries. This is the layer that captures obligations from email, corporate context, and conversations — the inputs that a simple task list would miss entirely.

Components:
1. Email Intake Agent: Gmail → classify (meeting invite / deadline / implicit ask / noise) → extract → World State. Claude-powered.
2. Corporate Bridge: iCal URL subscription for work calendar + optional email forwarding rules. Partially rule-based.
3. Conversation Intake Agent: Claude session → extract tasks/commitments/decisions → confidence-gate → Beads issues or Supabase tasks.

Key principle: source determines destination. Work brainstorming → work Beads issues. Personal planning → life tasks. No manual labelling.

Depends on: EPIC-INTEL (output flows through Rules Engine into World State)

See ARD v2 §Intake Layer as a First-Class Architectural Concern and §Conversation as Work Stream.

title: Implement basic recommendation engine
description: Algorithm that suggests which tasks to work on next based on deadlines, time available, and priority.
design: Three categories: urgent (deadline < 3 days), quick wins (timeRequired < 1h), big blocks (timeRequired >= 3h). Score and rank tasks. Return top 3 recommendations with reason.
close_reason: Scoring algorithm: deadline urgency (+100/60/30), priority boost (+40/20/5), quick wins <60m (+25), focus block fit (+20). Returns top 3.

title: Add calendar event display (mock)
description: Display upcoming calendar events alongside tasks so recommendations can account for scheduled time.

title: Audit and fix per-user recommendation cache key in recommend.js
description:
recommend.js uses a server-side 30-minute cache. If the cache is keyed globally (or not keyed at all), a second user making a request on a warm cache will receive the first user's recommendations. With multi-user support active, this is a data leakage bug.

Audit the current cache implementation in recommend.js. If the cache is a plain module-level variable, change the cache key to include userId so each user has an independent cache entry. The 30-minute TTL behaviour should remain unchanged.
design:
## Scenarios

- Given user A triggers a recommendation refresh and the response is cached
  When user B makes a fresh request while the cache is still warm
  Then cache lookup finds no entry for user B's userId, a new Claude call is made for user B

- Given user A has a warm cache entry
  When user A makes a second request within 30 minutes
  Then cache hit returns user A's recommendations without a new Claude call

- Given the cache has an entry for user A's userId
  When 30+ minutes pass and user A makes another request
  Then cache miss, new Claude call made, response re-cached under user A's userId
close_reason: Audit complete. Cache is entirely Supabase-backed (recommendation_history table) queried with .eq('user_id', userId) on every request. No module-level variable. Per-user isolation is automatic once userId comes from JWT — no code changes needed.

title: Update calendar-sync-scheduled to loop over all users (multi-user support)
description:
calendar-sync-scheduled.js currently hardcodes a single SUPABASE_USER_ID to sync Google and iCal calendars. Once multi-user auth is in place (Hardening Phase 2), a second user who connects their calendar would never get synced by the nightly job.

Fix: Replace the single-user env var read with a query to user_preferences using the service role key. Fetch all rows, then filter in-memory for users with google_refresh_token or non-empty ical_feeds array. Loop and run syncGoogle + syncICal for each active user. Wrap each user's sync in try/catch so one user's expired token doesn't abort the entire job.

SUPABASE_USER_ID env var can be removed from the scheduled function context after this change.
design:
## Scenarios

- Given two users exist in user_preferences, both with google_refresh_token set
  When calendar-sync-scheduled cron fires
  Then syncGoogle runs for both users, both calendar_snapshot tables are updated

- Given one user has an invalid/expired Google refresh token
  When syncGoogle fails for that user
  Then error is logged for that user, the loop continues, the other user's sync completes successfully

- Given a user row has google_refresh_token=null and ical_feeds=[]
  When the active user filter runs
  Then that user is excluded from the sync loop, no unnecessary token refresh attempted
close_reason: Scheduled function now queries all user_preferences rows via service role, filters in memory for users with google_refresh_token or non-empty ical_feeds, loops with per-user try/catch. SUPABASE_USER_ID env var no longer used. Committed in 3e17fc3.

title: Unified task list: manual tasks + Beads interleaved
description:
## Why

Manual tasks (open_tasks), Beads issues (beads_ready), and eventually email-intake tasks
all live in separate UI sections. The user sees three separate lists instead of one
prioritized inbox. The data is already normalised to a common shape — all three sources
produce objects with {id, title, status, priority, deadline, source, ...}.

## What

Replace the separate Tasks section and Beads section with a single unified task list,
sorted by: overdue first → deadline ascending → priority → source. Each row still shows
its source badge (manual / beads / email). The QuickStats counts should span all sources.

The separate BeadsTaskRow accordion (with description/notes/commands) can be preserved as
an expand-on-click panel inside the unified list.

## Scenarios

- Given manual tasks and Beads issues both exist
  When the app loads
  Then all tasks appear in a single list, sorted by urgency/priority

- Given a task is overdue and a Beads issue has no deadline
  When the list renders
  Then the overdue task appears above the Beads issue

- Given the user clicks a Beads task row
  When it expands
  Then the description, notes, and terminal commands panel is shown (existing BeadsTaskRow behaviour)

- Given no tasks or Beads issues exist
  When the list renders
  Then a single empty state message is shown
close_reason: Superseded by life-at4 tabs implementation.

title: Schedule task as Google Calendar event
description:
## Why

Tasks have a timeRequired field but no way to block time for them. The Google Calendar
OAuth infrastructure (auth-google-refresh, the enabled Calendar API, stored refresh token)
is already in place. We should let users turn any task into a calendar event with one tap.

## What

Add a "Schedule →" button to TaskRow. Tapping it posts to a new schedule-task Netlify
function that fetches a fresh Google access token, creates a calendar event with the task
title and timeRequired duration, and stores the returned event ID back on the task
(source_url or a new calendar_event_id column).

The UI should show a calendar badge on tasks that have a linked event.

## Scenarios

- Given a task with timeRequired set
  When the user taps "Schedule →"
  Then a calendar event is created with the task title and timeRequired duration, starting
  at the next available hour

- Given a task without timeRequired
  When the user taps "Schedule →"
  Then a default 30-minute event is created

- Given a task already linked to a calendar event (has calendar_event_id)
  When the user views the task
  Then a calendar badge/link is shown instead of the "Schedule →" button

- Given the calendar sync runs after scheduling
  When the app checks for today's events
  Then the newly scheduled event appears in the Calendar section
notes:
## Design decisions

- New column: calendar_event_url text on open_tasks (stores Google Calendar htmlLink)
- Start time: next full hour from now (no picker for MVP — user moves it in GCal)
- Duration: task.timeRequired minutes, default 30 if unset
- Token refresh: inline (same pattern as calendar-sync.js, not via auth-google-refresh HTTP)
- Column probe: schedule-task.js probes for calendar_event_url at runtime (graceful if migration not yet run — event still created, just no badge)
- Prop thread: scheduleTask flows LifeOrganizer → useTasks → UnifiedTaskList → TaskRow
- Badge: 📅 link (opens GCal) replaces Schedule button once calendarEventUrl is set on task
- Scope: TaskRow only (manual tasks); BeadsTaskRow unchanged

## New files
- supabase/migrations/20260523000000_open_tasks_add_calendar_event_url.sql
- netlify/functions/schedule-task.js

## Changed files
- netlify/functions/tasks.js (probe + fromDbRow for calendar_event_url)
- src/LifeOrganizer.jsx (scheduleTask in useTasks, onSchedule prop, TaskRow button)

Work week: Mon-Fri of current week, grouped by day header, events as time-sorted list per day — no pixel-positioned grid needed. title: Netlify scheduled function for urgent task email notifications
description:
Server-side hourly check for tasks with deadline approaching, sends email digest. Requires Supabase (tasks must be server-accessible — localStorage is client-only). Prerequisite: Supabase integration must be complete.

Implementation Plan:
1. Install dependencies in netlify/functions/: npm install @supabase/supabase-js resend.
2. Create netlify/functions/notify-urgent.mts.
3. In netlify.toml add: [functions.notify-urgent] schedule = "0 * * * *" (runs hourly).
4. Function logic: init Supabase with service key (bypasses RLS). Query tasks WHERE deadline < NOW() + INTERVAL '24 hours' AND status = 'pending', joined to auth.users for email address.
5. Check notifications_sent table (task_id, sent_at) — skip tasks already notified. Send remaining via Resend API (resend.com — free tier 3000/month): subject 'Due tomorrow: <title>', plain text body with title, deadline, and bd close hint.
6. Insert sent records into notifications_sent to prevent duplicate emails on the next run.
7. Required Netlify env vars: RESEND_API_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY, SUPABASE_URL.
8. Depends on: life-bok (Supabase integration must be live first).
design: 1. npm install @supabase/supabase-js resend (in netlify/functions/). 2. Create netlify/functions/notify-urgent.mts. In netlify.toml add: [functions.notify-urgent]\nschedule = '0 * * * *'. 3. Function logic: init Supabase with service key (bypasses RLS). Query: SELECT tasks.*, auth.users.email FROM tasks JOIN auth.users ON tasks.user_id=auth.users.id WHERE deadline < NOW()+INTERVAL '24 hours' AND status='pending'. 4. For each result, send via Resend API (resend.com — free tier 3000/month, simple REST API): subject 'Due tomorrow: <title>', plain text body with title + deadline + bd close hint. 5. Track sent notifications in a notifications_sent table (task_id, sent_at) to avoid duplicate emails. 6. Env vars needed in Netlify dashboard: RESEND_API_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY, SUPABASE_URL.
close_reason: Superseded by EPIC-NOTIFY. Email dispatch now flows through the Rules Engine → Notification Dispatcher pattern rather than a standalone Netlify cron. Replaced by Email Dispatch sub-issue under the Notification & Dispatch epic.

title: Supabase integration for cross-device task storage
description:
Manual tasks live in localStorage only — no cross-device sync, no server-side access for scheduled notifications, and lost if browser data is cleared. Supabase provides PostgreSQL with Row Level Security, a JS client, and magic-link auth — unlocking cross-device sync and unblocking server-side email notifications.

Implementation Plan:
1. Create Supabase project at supabase.com. Run schema: CREATE TABLE tasks (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, user_id UUID REFERENCES auth.users, title TEXT, category TEXT, priority TEXT, time_required INT, deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT, source TEXT DEFAULT 'manual', source_url TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()); ALTER TABLE tasks ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; CREATE POLICY 'own tasks' ON tasks USING (auth.uid()=user_id) WITH CHECK (auth.uid()=user_id).
2. npm install @supabase/supabase-js.
3. Add VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY to .env.local (gitignored) and to Netlify env vars.
4. Create useSupabaseTasks hook that mirrors the current useState+localStorage pattern but reads/writes to Supabase.
5. Auth: supabase.auth.signInWithOtp({email}) magic link. Minimal UI: email input + 'Send link' button, shown when not authenticated.
6. Migration path: on first Supabase login, if localStorage has tasks, offer a one-click import button.
7. Graceful degradation: if not logged in, fall back to localStorage (existing behavior unchanged).

title: Design and generate real PWA icons
description:
Current icons are placeholder blue circles generated with Python. PWA install prompt and home screen show these — embarrassing for a tool you actually use daily. Need real branded icons before sharing or using the app seriously.

Implementation Plan:
1. Design the icon: 'LO' monogram or a calendar/organizer glyph on blue-600 (#2563eb) background. Must be readable at 48px and sharp at 512px. Create SVG as the canonical source file.
2. Export PNG assets: 192×192 → public/icon-192.png, 512×512 → public/icon-512.png, 180×180 → public/apple-touch-icon.png. Tools: Figma (free), Canva, or hand-coded SVG + Inkscape/sharp for PNG export.
3. Add apple-touch-icon to index.html <head>: <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">.
4. Add theme-color meta if not present: <meta name="theme-color" content="#2563eb">.
5. Verify vite-plugin-pwa config / public/manifest.json references the correct icon paths and sizes.
6. Test PWA install prompt on iOS Safari and Android Chrome — confirm home screen icon renders correctly at both sizes.

notes: MANIFEST LOCATION: vite-plugin-pwa generates the manifest dynamically from vite.config.ts — there is no static public/manifest.json. Icons are defined in the plugins([VitePWA({ manifest: { icons: [...] } })]) section of vite.config.ts. To update icon paths or add apple-touch-icon, edit vite.config.ts (not a JSON file). APPLE-TOUCH-ICON: still needs to be added to index.html <head> — vite-plugin-pwa does not inject it automatically. Add: <link rel='apple-touch-icon' href='/apple-touch-icon.png'>. THEME COLOR: already present in vite.config.ts manifest as '#2563eb'.

value: Never hand action items back to the user when Claude has the tools to complete them. If a gap is identified and the fix is within reach (file edit, env var, shell config, SQL to run, etc.), close it immediately. 'Action item for you' is a cop-out.

Always use 'bdg' commands for issue creates, closes, updates — NOT bare 'bd'. bdg routes writes to the correct project repo AND auto-runs bd repo sync + DoltHub push in the background, keeping Railway and the PWA in sync. Use: 'bdg create --project life-organizer ...' not 'bd create ...'. Bare 'bd' skips the sync chain entirely.

NEVER merge a PR without explicit user instruction. Create the PR, post the URL, stop. The user decides when and whether to merge. This is especially critical for auth/gating changes — a broken AuthGuard, middleware, or login flow is a production denial: nobody can get in and there is no recovery without another deploy. The correct path for high-risk changes: branch deploy preview → verify end-to-end → create PR → wait for user to merge.

Always pass --labels <project> when running bd create, where <project> is this project's label (matches the issue_prefix from bd config list, e.g. 'life-organizer', 'mnews', 'heroheaven'). Every issue must carry the project label so bdg list --label-pattern works correctly in beads-global. Check the label with: bd config list | grep issue_prefix
BEADS_API_KEY is correctly set on Railway (verified 2026-05-22). Value matches .zshrc and .env.local. Previous mismatch is resolved — no action needed.

Netlify MCP manage-env-vars upsertEnvVar silently fails — says 'upserted' but var never appears. Always use 'netlify env:set' CLI instead. Secrets require --context flag (e.g. --context production,branch-deploy,deploy-preview).

Process: end-to-end verification for multi-system beads. Any bead whose acceptance scenario crosses more than one system (local Dolt → DoltHub → Railway → Supabase → PWA, or local code → Netlify → external API) must be verified at the FINAL consumer, not at the point of implementation. 'Stamp written', 'curl fired', 'function returned 200', 'hook ran' are all intermediate signals — not acceptance. The real test is: does the end user experience (PWA, UI, downstream service) reflect the change? Close only after verifying the last step in the chain. Corollary: write acceptance scenarios that name the final consumer explicitly, not the implementation artifact.
